Summary:
This paper presented a local causal dynamic learning method by introducing meaningful events. It consists of two parts (1) inferencing local causal graph through quantization and (2) predicting next state. Empirical result on two RL environments showed the effectiveness of the proposed learning method. More importantly it also showed identifiability result for the proposed method.

Strengths:
1. The assumption that separating the whole (s,a) space into decomposition is intuitive.
2. This paper provided identifiability result of the proposed method that the optimal decomposition that maximizes the score identifies a meaningful context that exhibits fine-grained causal relationships.
3. The experiments (Chemical and Magnetic) showed the effectiveness of the proposed method.

Weaknesses:
* The theory and the implementation seem not aligned.
  * In theory there are ground truth decomposition which the LCG are the same within partition and different otherwise, however, it seems that for Magnetic, there is a uniform causal graph which does not change locally. 
  * Identifiability results are not reflected in the method. There is no quantitative result that supporting that identifiability is or is not achieved in the experiment. 
  * Unclear relation between ID, OOD setting with the decomposition and the codebook size K (see questions).
* The assumption on the decomposition is not mild. In real world data, the transition function is usually highly complex. This method can only handle the case when different parts in the decomposition have different LCG. However, in real case, all parts may share the same LCG due to the highly complex transition function. Even though the conditional independence relations are the same (same LCG), the transition function itself may be used to further partition the sample space into further small parts. That is saying same LCG but with different $p(s'\vert s,a)$.

Questions:
* Minor presentation issues about the notations.
  * In Sec 3.1, what is the definition of event $\mathcal{E}$?
  * In terms of the score function $\mathcal{S}(\\{\mathcal{G}_z,\mathcal{E}_z\\}_1^K):=\text{sup }\mathbb{E}\left[\text{log}\hat{p}(s' \vert s,a;\mathcal{G}_z) - \lambda \vert \mathcal{G}_z\vert\right]$

* For ID setting, the proposed method is not the best, is there any explanation for this?
* How to decide the number of codebook vector K?
* For the case mentioned in Weakness, when dealing with more complex data, if the LCG is shared but the transition function is indeed different among partitions, is the proposed method flexible enough to generalize to those case?

reviews.text:
Review for “Quantized Local Independence Discovery for Fine-Grained Causal Dynamics Learning in Reinforcement Learning”

Summary:  
This paper proposes a novel framework for learning event-specific causal dynamics models in factored Markov decision processes (MDPs) by partitioning the joint state–action space into a finite set of “events” and discovering, via vector quantization, a discrete latent code for each event together with the corresponding local causal graph (LCG). The key idea is to learn a codebook of discrete prototypes \(e_z\) (for \(z\in[K]\)) that (i) assigns each sample \((s,a)\) to exactly one event, (ii) decodes into an adjacency mask \(A_z\) representing local independences (i.e., fine-grained causal relationships for that event), and (iii) uses only the locally relevant parent variables \(Pa(j,z)\) to predict each next-state factor \(s'_j\). The authors derive a regularized maximum-likelihood score (Eq. (4)) whose maximizer provably recovers meaningful contexts exhibiting event-specific causal structure (Propositions 2–3, Theorem 1). A practical end-to-end learning algorithm is implemented via a VQ-VAE–style codebook (Eqs. (5)–(7)) and Gumbel-Softmax relaxation for adjacency masks. Empirical results on two controlled RL environments—Chemical (full-fork and full-chain variants) and Magnetic—demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms dense, modular, graph-based, and prior causal baselines in robustness and generalization to OOD test states with added noise. Furthermore, visualizations of inferred code usage and LCGs confirm that the method indeed discovers meaningful events (e.g., fork structure when the root node’s color = red in Chemical, or non-magnetic context in Magnetic) and discards redundant dependencies.

Strengths:  
1. The core assumption—decomposing the joint \((s,a)\) domain into a small number of subgroups (“events”) under which local independence relationships hold—is both intuitive and well motivated by practical scenarios (e.g., lane-change only matters when no nearby cars). By formulating event discovery as part of a unified score maximization (Sec 3.2), the paper offers a conceptually clean extension of global causal learning to a fine-grained setting.  
2. Theoretical contributions are significant: the paper proves that the decomposition and local graphs maximizing the regularized likelihood score (Eq (4)) indeed identify contexts with sparser, event-specific causal graphs (Propositions 2–3) and establishes identifiability in factored MDPs (Theorem 1). These results build on standard SCM assumptions (Markov, faithfulness, sufficiency) but go further by considering context-specific independence.  
3. The empirical evaluation on Chemical and Magnetic tasks is thorough. Tables 1–2 show consistent downstream improvements—especially under OOD noise levels—over baselines including MLP, modular nets, GNN, NPS, CDL, GRADER, and NCD. Visualization of codebook histograms (Figs 4–5) and recovered LCGs clearly illustrate that the approach discovers the correct fork/chain structures and ignores spurious variables under non-magnetic contexts. Ablations over codebook size \(K\) (Table 3) further demonstrate robustness to the choice of \(K>1\).

Weaknesses:  
* Alignment between theory and practice is not fully clear.  
  – The theoretical framework assumes that each partition \(\mathcal E_z\) corresponds to a ground-truth event where the local graph is constant, and different events yield different LCGs. Yet in the Magnetic environment the “true” causal graph is global (Fig 7 (a)) with only certain edges becoming redundant under a non-magnetic event—there is no change in skeleton, only edge irrelevance. This uniformity conflicts with the assumption of distinct LCG skeletons across events.  
  – Although Theorem 1 guarantees identifiability in principle, there is no quantitative metric (e.g., SHD vs. ground-truth LCG) reported in the experiments to verify that the learned decomposition and graphs match the theoretical optimum. The paper provides only qualitative plots of code usage and mask patterns.  
  – The relationship between in-distribution (ID) vs. OOD settings (adding Gaussian noise to node observations in Chemical, relocating box outside training range in Magnetic) and the number of codebook vectors \(K\) is not fully clarified. It is unclear how many events are truly needed to cover the test perturbations, or whether increasing \(K\) beyond the number of semantic contexts adds redundant partitions.  
* Assumption on decomposition may be too strong for real-world data.  
  – The method requires that different parts of the state–action space exhibit different conditional-independence patterns. In more complex domains, the LCG skeleton may remain constant everywhere, while the actual conditional densities \(p(s' \mid s,a)\) vary continuously, suggesting a need for partitions by function rather than by structure. The current approach cannot further subdivide when LCGs coincide but transition functions differ, limiting applicability.  
  – Even when the skeleton is constant, one could imagine subsets of data where \(p(s' \mid Pa(j),z)\) shifts subtly (e.g., due to multimodal dynamics) yet without changing independences. The discrete codebook approach (Eqs (5)–(7)) might struggle to capture such smooth variation.

Questions and Clarifications:  
* Notational clarity in Sec 3.1: the event \(\mathcal E\subset X\) appears without formal definition. It would help to state explicitly that an event is a measurable subset of the joint space \(\mathcal X=S\times A\) with \(p(\mathcal E)>0\).  
* In the score function \( \mathcal S(\{\mathcal G_z,\mathcal E_z\}_{z=1}^K) := \sup \mathbb E[\log \hat p(s'\mid s,a; \mathcal G_z) - \lambda|\mathcal G_z|]\) (Eq (4)), which variables are optimized by \(\sup\)? Is it over the dynamics model parameters \(\phi\) and the graphs \(\mathcal G_z\)? Also, what is the expectation \(\mathbb E\) taken over—joint \((s,a,s')\) or conditional \(p(s,a)\)?  
* In Proposition 3’s proof (App B.2), the step “the last equality holds” (Eq (39) → Eq (40)) requires elaboration. More detail on how the score difference \(\mathcal S(\mathcal G) - \mathcal S(\hat{\mathcal G})\) expands into KL divergences plus penalties would aid readers.  
* The theory posits a ground-truth decomposition \(\{\mathcal E_z, G_z\}\). What are these for Chemical full-fork and Magnetic? In Chemical full-fork, one hopes to recover a red-root event vs. its complement, but for Magnetic the two events (“both red” vs. “at least one black”) share the same skeleton, raising doubt about the “true” distinct LCGs.  
* How might one measure whether identifiability is achieved during training? A metric such as mean Structural Hamming Distance between inferred mask \(A_z\) and the oracle LCG on held-out event-labeled data could be introduced.  
* On in-distribution (ID) tasks, the proposed method is sometimes not best (Table 1 shows slight drops vs. Oracle or CDL). Can the authors comment on why quantization overhead yields minor performance degradation when no noise is present?  
* How should a practitioner choose the codebook size \(K\)? Beyond the simple ablation (Table 3), is there a rule of thumb based on expected number of semantic contexts or elbow-method using validation loss?  
* Finally, if in a real scenario the skeleton remains globally constant but \(p(s'\mid s,a)\) itself varies, could the method be extended—e.g., by allowing event-specific neural nets even with identical mask \(A_z\)? Would that address the limitation noted above?

Overall, this work makes an interesting theoretical and practical contribution by integrating event-level partitioning into causal dynamics learning, but the alignment of assumptions, theory, and empirical setup could be strengthened. Minor clarifications on notation and additional quantitative evaluation of identifiability would further solidify the paper.  

Rating: 6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper proposed a novel method that discovers meaningful events and infers fine-grained causal relationships. They recommend learning a discrete latent variable representing the pair of events and causal graphs via vector quantization. Experimental results demonstrate their method is more robust to unseen states.
reviews.strengths:
1.	The discovery of fine-grained event-based causality is novel and intuitive, and the experimental results also prove the author's point of view.
2.	The authors theoretically prove that their grouping method does not depend on the hyperparameter k setting and that meaningful context can be discovered in each group.
3.	Figures 4 and 5 in the experimental results clearly demonstrate the advantages of their event-based method in discovering fine-grained causal relationships.
reviews.weaknesses: 1.	The validation environment for the experiment is somewhat simple, and the assumption of full observables seems to have significant limitations.
reviews.questions:
1.	In Table 3, it seems that the larger K is, the better the performance is. What happens if K is increased to equal the number of samples?
2.	Intuitively, an event should be a sequence of continuous states and actions. I would like to ask whether Eq. (7) is more inclined to aggregate neighboring states?

This paper proposed a novel method that discovers meaningful events and infers fine-grained causal relationships. They recommend learning a discrete latent variable representing the pair of events and causal graphs via vector quantization. Experimental results demonstrate their method is more robust to unseen states.

Strengths:
1.	The discovery of fine-grained event-based causality is novel and intuitive, and the experimental results also prove the author's point of view.
2.	The authors theoretically prove that their grouping method does not depend on the hyperparameter k setting and that meaningful context can be discovered in each group.
3.	Figures 4 and 5 in the experimental results clearly demonstrate the advantages of their event-based method in discovering fine-grained causal relationships.

Weaknesses:

Questions:

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a gradient-based method for local-independence-based causal discovery, and applies it to the Causal Dynamics Modeling problem in model-based reinforcement learning. Following (Hwang et al. 2023), the basic idea is to partition the state-action space into subsets, each called an "event" in this paper, and then to learn the transition function of the MDP as a collection of event-specific causal models. The main difference from prior work is that the event partitioning in this paper is learned through a clustering procedure over a (jointly-learned) latent embedding of the state-action space, using Eq.7. With two specially-designed MDP environments, the paper experimentally shows that MDP planning powered by dynamics model learned from their method performs more robustly under some out-of-distribution settings. Finally, the paper also presents some theoretical results about the soundness of jointly optimizing the event partitioning scheme and the event-specific causal models.
reviews.strengths: I think the paper touches an interesting and important topic. Causal discovery, for whatever reason we want to do it, is often hindered by the fact that many causal effects only manifest themselves under certain conditions. So, targeting at learning local models that each only works for a specific condition, instead of at learning a generic model, seems to be a promising direction for causal discovery in general. The problem of collaboratively learning the condition-specific models and the conditions that effectively facilitate such model discovery, is however quite challenging, and is attacked in this paper. It is also nice to see that the paper embeds this causal discovery task into model-based RL and experimentally tests its application in this scenario.
reviews.weaknesses:
**(a)** I am not sure about the soundness of the proposed method. The paper claims that they are jointly optimizing both the event decomposition/partitioning scheme and the causal models, but I doubt if the learning of the decomposition scheme is indeed effectively signaled by the impact of the decomposition on the quality of the resulted causal models. Moreover, I doubt if the learning objective designed for optimizing decomposition (Eq.7) may lead to degenerate results. See my question 1 and 2 below for the detailed concerns. Since joint optimization is a main selling point of this paper (without it the theory part becomes largely irrelevant to the experiment part, for example), this is a serious issue that must be clarified.

**(b)** I have concern on the reproducibility of the experiment part. No pseudo-code is provided for the proposed method, and the method description in Section 3.3, 4.1.2, and C.2, missed some important pieces. For example, how is the data sampled (what's the exploration policy being used)? Exactly what is being updated by L_pred and L_quant (the gradient is respective to what)? What are exactly computed for the differentiation?
  
Moreover, the experimentation code is not given either, and the description of the two MDP environments are not detailed enough for third-parties to reproduce the experiment, I'm afraid. For example, how exactly the nodes in the Chemical environment affect each other? How exactly the noise is injected at testing time? In the robot-arm environment, how exactly the "unseen locations" of the box is determined at testing time? In Section C.1.3, it's said that at training time the positions of the box are "randomly sampled within the range of the table", does this mean every location on the table has a positive probability density to be selected at training time (If so, the location of the box at testing time is not really out-of-distribution, even though it may be unseen)?   

**(c)** I have concerns on the theory part too. On one hand, the main theorem seems to only apply to a rather special case (where the causal model can only have two irreducible phases). On the other hand, the lemmas (Prop. 1-3) used for proving the main theorems seem to have flawed statements and proofs. Some important assumptions are not explicitly stated. See my question 3~8 for the details in this regard.

**(d)** The paper may need to better contrast to prior work, especially to (Hwang et al., 2023). It seems (Hwang et al., 2023) is not limited to sample-specific decomposition, but also discusses event-level or event-set-level decomposition. Proposition 1 in the paper under review is essentially a rephrasing of Proposition 4 in (Hwang et al., 2023), for example.

---  post-rebuttal edits ---

After the rebuttal I have less concerns in all the four aspects above, but not to the extent that I'm confident to say that the issues have been resolved. Overall, I raise my recommendation to 5 (marginally reject); I think it may be beneficial to have another round of revision and review for this paper, although I won't strongly object to accepting the current draft right away.
reviews.questions:
1. In Eq.6, will the gradient of L_pred be used to update the codebook $C$ and (parameters in) the embedding model $h$? If I understand correctly, the decomposition scheme mainly depends on the codebook $C$ and the embedding model $h$, and both of them affect L_pred through $e$, via Eq.5 (and $e$ affects L_pred further through $A$). However, how does the gradient propagates through the argmin operator in Eq.5? 

(Suppose the gradient of L_pred does not change $C$ and $h$, then the learning of the decomposition scheme actually does not take into account its consequence on the likelihood score, but is solely based on the gradient of L_quant, in this case the algorithm you proposed is not truly a joint optimization method, although the decomposition and the causal models are indeed learned "in parallel".)

2. I don't quite get the rationale behind Eq.7. It seems to me that the L_quant defined in Eq.7 wants to encourage the feature vector $h$ of the state-action and its corresponding cluster center $e$ to be close to each other. But is this enough to learn a "good" decomposition? As a trivial and degenerate solution, imagine the embedding model h always outputs a constant vector, and all the cluster center $e_z$ in the codebook also equal this constant vector, this will minimize L_quant to zero, but is clearly not what we want. This question is assuming that the answer to Question 1 above is no (that the learning of $C$ and $h$ are not further based on the likelihood loss L_pred).

3. I can't quite follow the proof of Proposition 2. In fact, I'm not sure if the proposition is exactly correct as there is no requirement on the value of lambda at all. In comparison, Lemma 1 requires *small enough* (yet non-zero) lambda. Does Prop.2 also require special lambda values? For continuous state and action spaces, there can be an uncountably infinite number of possible decomposition schemes, each may require a different "small enough" lambda for the regularized likelihood score to work, in this case do we still have a single lambda that works for all decompositions? In general, the role of lambda seems to be subtle yet crucial for the entire theory developed here. 

4. In the proof of Proposition 3, I'm not sure about the inequality at line 4 of the proof -- between the equality sign, why can the likelihood terms $\hat{p}$ in S be dropped?

5. The probability factorization at Line 1 of Page 3 implies that you are assuming all the $S'_j$'s are independent to each other (conditioned on given $s,a$), right? Which propositions proved in the paper need this assumption? Without this assumption, the causal model is not necessarily bipartite, and skeleton learning based on local independence would be not enough, and we perhaps don't have unique identifiability any more? Also, does the environments used in your experiment satisfy this assumption? 

6. Please explicitly and formally state all the external propositions that your proofs crucially depend on and yet are only proved in other literature, such as (Hwang et al. 2023, Prop. 4) and (Brouillard et al. 2020, Thm. 1).

7. You define "local causal model" based on the concept of PA(j; E), but how is PA(j;E) defined? Does the global causal model entail the local models through the induced local independence relationships? In that case a proper *definition* of local causal model would be entirely conditioned on the E-faithfulness assumption, am I right?

8. In the proof of Lemma 1 you mentioned some assumptions and said they are assumed "throughout the paper". Such assumptions should not be placed in the appendix, in the middle of a proof for a proposition. They should at least be given in the statement of the involved propositions, in the main text. 

9. Is your algorithm equivalent to NCD if we set $K=|S \times A|$?
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposes a gradient-based method for local-independence-based causal discovery, and applies it to the Causal Dynamics Modeling problem in model-based reinforcement learning. Following (Hwang et al. 2023), the basic idea is to partition the state-action space into subsets, each called an "event" in this paper, and then to learn the transition function of the MDP as a collection of event-specific causal models. The main difference from prior work is that the event partitioning in this paper is learned through a clustering procedure over a (jointly-learned) latent embedding of the state-action space, using Eq.7. With two specially-designed MDP environments, the paper experimentally shows that MDP planning powered by dynamics model learned from their method performs more robustly under some out-of-distribution settings. Finally, the paper also presents some theoretical results about the soundness of jointly optimizing the event partitioning scheme and the event-specific causal models.

Strengths:
I think the paper touches an interesting and important topic. Causal discovery, for whatever reason we want to do it, is often hindered by the fact that many causal effects only manifest themselves under certain conditions. So, targeting at learning local models that each only works for a specific condition, instead of at learning a generic model, seems to be a promising direction for causal discovery in general. The problem of collaboratively learning the condition-specific models and the conditions that effectively facilitate such model discovery, is however quite challenging, and is attacked in this paper. It is also nice to see that the paper embeds this causal discovery task into model-based RL and experimentally tests its application in this scenario.

Weaknesses:
**(a)** I am not sure about the soundness of the proposed method. The paper claims that they are jointly optimizing both the event decomposition/partitioning scheme and the causal models, but I doubt if the learning of the decomposition scheme is indeed effectively signaled by the impact of the decomposition on the quality of the resulted causal models. Moreover, I doubt if the learning objective designed for optimizing decomposition (Eq.7) may lead to degenerate results. See my question 1 and 2 below for the detailed concerns. Since joint optimization is a main selling point of this paper (without it the theory part becomes largely irrelevant to the experiment part, for example), this is a serious issue that must be clarified.

**(b)** I have concern on the reproducibility of the experiment part. No pseudo-code is provided for the proposed method, and the method description in Section 3.3, 4.1.2, and C.2, missed some important pieces. For example, how is the data sampled (what's the exploration policy being used)? Exactly what is being updated by L_pred and L_quant (the gradient is respective to what)? What are exactly computed for the differentiation?
  
Moreover, the experimentation code is not given either, and the description of the two MDP environments are not detailed enough for third-parties to reproduce the experiment, I'm afraid. For example, how exactly the nodes in the Chemical environment affect each other? How exactly the noise is injected at testing time? In the robot-arm environment, how exactly the "unseen locations" of the box is determined at testing time? In Section C.1.3, it's said that at training time the positions of the box are "randomly sampled within the range of the table", does this mean every location on the table has a positive probability density to be selected at training time (If so, the location of the box at testing time is not really out-of-distribution, even though it may be unseen)?   

**(c)** I have concerns on the theory part too. On one hand, the main theorem seems to only apply to a rather special case (where the causal model can only have two irreducible phases). On the other hand, the lemmas (Prop. 1-3) used for proving the main theorems seem to have flawed statements and proofs. Some important assumptions are not explicitly stated. See my question 3~8 for the details in this regard.

**(d)** The paper may need to better contrast to prior work, especially to (Hwang et al., 2023). It seems (Hwang et al., 2023) is not limited to sample-specific decomposition, but also discusses event-level or event-set-level decomposition. Proposition 1 in the paper under review is essentially a rephrasing of Proposition 4 in (Hwang et al., 2023), for example.

---  post-rebuttal edits ---

After the rebuttal I have less concerns in all the four aspects above, but not to the extent that I'm confident to say that the issues have been resolved. Overall, I raise my recommendation to 5 (marginally reject); I think it may be beneficial to have another round of revision and review for this paper, although I won't strongly object to accepting the current draft right away.

Questions:
1. In Eq.6, will the gradient of L_pred be used to update the codebook $C$ and (parameters in) the embedding model $h$? If I understand correctly, the decomposition scheme mainly depends on the codebook $C$ and the embedding model $h$, and both of them affect L_pred through $e$, via Eq.5 (and $e$ affects L_pred further through $A$). However, how does the gradient propagates through the argmin operator in Eq.5? 

(Suppose the gradient of L_pred does not change $C$ and $h$, then the learning of the decomposition scheme actually does not take into account its consequence on the likelihood score, but is solely based on the gradient of L_quant, in this case the algorithm you proposed is not truly a joint optimization method, although the decomposition and the causal models are indeed learned "in parallel".)

2. I don't quite get the rationale behind Eq.7. It seems to me that the L_quant defined in Eq.7 wants to encourage the feature vector $h$ of the state-action and its corresponding cluster center $e$ to be close to each other. But is this enough to learn a "good" decomposition? As a trivial and degenerate solution, imagine the embedding model h always outputs a constant vector, and all the cluster center $e_z$ in the codebook also equal this constant vector, this will minimize L_quant to zero, but is clearly not what we want. This question is assuming that the answer to Question 1 above is no (that the learning of $C$ and $h$ are not further based on the likelihood loss L_pred).

3. I can't quite follow the proof of Proposition 2. In fact, I'm not sure if the proposition is exactly correct as there is no requirement on the value of lambda at all. In comparison, Lemma 1 requires *small enough* (yet non-zero) lambda. Does Prop.2 also require special lambda values? For continuous state and action spaces, there can be an uncountably infinite number of possible decomposition schemes, each may require a different "small enough" lambda for the regularized likelihood score to work, in this case do we still have a single lambda that works for all decompositions? In general, the role of lambda seems to be subtle yet crucial for the entire theory developed here. 

4. In the proof of Proposition 3, I'm not sure about the inequality at line 4 of the proof -- between the equality sign, why can the likelihood terms $\hat{p}$ in S be dropped?

5. The probability factorization at Line 1 of Page 3 implies that you are assuming all the $S'_j$'s are independent to each other (conditioned on given $s,a$), right? Which propositions proved in the paper need this assumption? Without this assumption, the causal model is not necessarily bipartite, and skeleton learning based on local independence would be not enough, and we perhaps don't have unique identifiability any more? Also, does the environments used in your experiment satisfy this assumption? 

6. Please explicitly and formally state all the external propositions that your proofs crucially depend on and yet are only proved in other literature, such as (Hwang et al. 2023, Prop. 4) and (Brouillard et al. 2020, Thm. 1).

7. You define "local causal model" based on the concept of PA(j; E), but how is PA(j;E) defined? Does the global causal model entail the local models through the induced local independence relationships? In that case a proper *definition* of local causal model would be entirely conditioned on the E-faithfulness assumption, am I right?

8. In the proof of Lemma 1 you mentioned some assumptions and said they are assumed "throughout the paper". Such assumptions should not be placed in the appendix, in the middle of a proof for a proposition. They should at least be given in the statement of the involved propositions, in the main text. 

9. Is your algorithm equivalent to NCD if we set $K=|S \times A|$?

5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: LongLLMLingua: Accelerating and Enhancing LLMs in Long Context Scenarios via Prompt Compression

reviews.summary: The paper presents LongLLMLingua, a prompt compression method to improve the high computation cost, longer latency and inferior performance in current long context LLMs.
reviews.strengths:
1. the use of compression is well motivated.
2. the use of reordering mechanism is a promising method to solve the lost in the middle phenomenon.
3. the evaluation is throughout and comprehensive, especially Table 1 provides a fair and informative comparison, by using Chat-gpt and LongChat as target models (representative from close-sourced and open-sourced family).
reviews.weaknesses: 1. The latency evaluation setup is not well presented. The latency of API calls may have a high variance. Are the experiments conducted several times, and in different hours? If not, the reviewer suggests improving this and update the evaluation setup.
reviews.questions: Please address the weakness above.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper presents LongLLMLingua, a prompt compression method to improve the high computation cost, longer latency and inferior performance in current long context LLMs.

Strengths:
1. the use of compression is well motivated.
2. the use of reordering mechanism is a promising method to solve the lost in the middle phenomenon.
3. the evaluation is throughout and comprehensive, especially Table 1 provides a fair and informative comparison, by using Chat-gpt and LongChat as target models (representative from close-sourced and open-sourced family).

Weaknesses:
1. The latency evaluation setup is not well presented. The latency of API calls may have a high variance. Are the experiments conducted several times, and in different hours? If not, the reviewer suggests improving this and update the evaluation setup.

Questions:
Please address the weakness above.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Expanded Review

Summary  
This paper introduces LongLLMLingua, a novel prompt-compression framework designed to tackle three key challenges of large language models (LLMs) operating over long contexts: high computational and financial cost, increased end-to-end latency, and degraded task performance when relevant information is sparse or positioned suboptimally. The authors build upon the LLMLingua backbone, enriching it with four new components: a question-aware coarse-to-fine compression strategy (§4.1), a document reordering mechanism (§4.2) to mitigate the “lost in the middle” effect (Figure 1b), dynamic compression ratios (§4.3) for adaptive budget allocation, and a post-compression subsequence recovery step (§4.4) to restore any key tokens inadvertently pruned. They evaluate on NaturalQuestions, LongBench, ZeroSCROLLS, MuSiQue, and LooGLE benchmarks, reporting up to 17.1% accuracy gains on NaturalQuestions with 4× fewer tokens, 1.4×–3.8× latency speedups, and per-1,000-sample cost reductions of $28.5 (LongBench) and $27.4 (ZeroSCROLLS).

Strengths  
1. Well-motivated compression approach.  The paper convincingly argues—citing Shi et al. (2023) (Figure 1a) and Liu et al. (2023) (Figure 1b)—that both information density and token position critically affect long-context LLM performance. By explicitly optimizing for question relevance (contrastive perplexity in Eq. 3) and integrating coarse-to-fine token selection, the method directly addresses the root causes of performance drop and cost blowup in prior entropy-only compression schemes like Selective-Context and original LLMLingua.  
2. Promising reordering mechanism.  The document reordering step (§4.2, Eq. 4) capitalizes on the empirical finding that LLMs struggle when crucial content resides mid-prompt. In NaturalQuestions evaluations (Table 1), simply reordering top-ranked passages to the front and back yields consistent accuracy gains, demonstrating a lightweight yet effective remedy for the “lost in the middle” phenomenon.  
3. Thorough, multi-model evaluation.  Results span both closed-source (GPT-3.5-Turbo-0613) and open-source (LongChat-13B-16k) targets, six task types (Multi-Doc QA, Summarization, Few-Shot, Code Completion, Synthetic, Multi-Hop QA, and Long Dependency QA), and multiple long-context benchmarks (LongBench, ZeroSCROLLS, MuSiQue, LooGLE). Tables 1–3 and 5–8 collectively show that LongLLMLingua not only outperforms retrieval-based baselines (BM25, SBERT, OpenAI Embedding) under 2×–10× compression constraints, but also beats or matches full-context performance with substantial token reduction, yielding up to 3.9× inference speedups (Table 1). The inclusion of economic cost analysis (§E, Table 9) further underscores real-world feasibility.  

Weakness  
1. Latency evaluation setup.  While the paper reports impressive end-to-end speedups (1.4×–3.8×) by measuring combined compression and API request times on a V100-32G server (§5, “Latency evaluation”), the experimental protocol for mitigating API-call variance is under-specified. Network jitter, server queueing, and diurnal load fluctuations can produce significant latency noise when calling GPT-3.5-Turbo across different hours or geographic regions. Although the authors note that they “averaged over multiple measurements,” it remains unclear how many runs were performed per setting, whether runs were randomized over the day, or if standard deviations were recorded. Without these details, readers cannot assess the statistical reliability of the reported speedups.

Suggestion for Improvement  
To strengthen the latency claims, I recommend the authors:  
• Clearly state the number of repeated runs for each configuration and the total sampling period (e.g., “20 runs over three days, between 8 AM and 8 PM PST”).  
• Report mean ± standard deviation or confidence intervals for the end-to-end latency measurements in the main text or an extended appendix table.  
• If possible, complement API-based timing with local inference benchmarks (e.g., using an open-source model in a controlled environment) to isolate model-processing time from network overhead.  

Addressing these points will give future practitioners greater confidence in the consistency of the speedups and help them plan deployments under production‐scale constraints.

Decision  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper introduces LongLLMLingua as an innovative solution for prompt compression aimed at reducing costs and latency. LongLLMLingua stands out for its ability to consider the content of a question during compression, focusing on key tokens to enhance performance in scenarios with lengthy contexts. To achieve this, the authors propose several significant contributions: a question-aware compression framework, a document reordering based on a newly proposed importance metric, dynamic compression ratios, and a post-compression recovery strategy.
reviews.strengths: I appreciate the logical structure and clarity of this paper. The authors present their motivations compellingly, and the proposed LongLLMLingua method is both intuitive and seemingly effective, as evidenced by the strong results reported.
reviews.weaknesses:
I have several questions and comments regarding the methods outlined in the paper that I hope the authors can address:

1. The foundational work upon which this research is built, LLMLingua, should be appropriately cited from [https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.05736](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.05736), where the authors are clearly identified. Citing it with "Anonymous" authors seems unusual.

2. It might be beneficial to consolidate the list of contributions into three primary ones. Several contributions, such as document reordering, information reduction loss ... etc (discussed in Sections 4.2-4.4), seem relatively minor individually. They might be more appropriately categorized as a set of techniques or "tricks" that collectively enhance the framework's performance and stability.

3. Figure 5 alone seems insufficient to establish the full effectiveness of the framework. To provide a more comprehensive understanding, Tables 1 and 3 should include E2E runtime data, particularly to offer perspective on the performance of retrieval-based methods.

4. The contrastive perplexity-based importance metric's effectiveness isn't entirely convincing. Figure 3 (b) presents just a single example from a QA dataset where key documents are positioned at the beginning. Wouldn’t it be more informative to compare the average contrastive perplexity of key documents against that of nonessential documents? Similarly, a comparison using standard perplexity might also be illuminating.

5. I believe there might be a typographical error: 'ground-true' should likely be corrected to "ground-truth."
reviews.questions: see weaknesses
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces LongLLMLingua as an innovative solution for prompt compression aimed at reducing costs and latency. LongLLMLingua stands out for its ability to consider the content of a question during compression, focusing on key tokens to enhance performance in scenarios with lengthy contexts. To achieve this, the authors propose several significant contributions: a question-aware compression framework, a document reordering based on a newly proposed importance metric, dynamic compression ratios, and a post-compression recovery strategy.

Strengths:
I appreciate the logical structure and clarity of this paper. The authors present their motivations compellingly, and the proposed LongLLMLingua method is both intuitive and seemingly effective, as evidenced by the strong results reported.

Weaknesses:
I have several questions and comments regarding the methods outlined in the paper that I hope the authors can address:

1. The foundational work upon which this research is built, LLMLingua, should be appropriately cited from [https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.05736](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.05736), where the authors are clearly identified. Citing it with "Anonymous" authors seems unusual.

2. It might be beneficial to consolidate the list of contributions into three primary ones. Several contributions, such as document reordering, information reduction loss ... etc (discussed in Sections 4.2-4.4), seem relatively minor individually. They might be more appropriately categorized as a set of techniques or "tricks" that collectively enhance the framework's performance and stability.

3. Figure 5 alone seems insufficient to establish the full effectiveness of the framework. To provide a more comprehensive understanding, Tables 1 and 3 should include E2E runtime data, particularly to offer perspective on the performance of retrieval-based methods.

4. The contrastive perplexity-based importance metric's effectiveness isn't entirely convincing. Figure 3 (b) presents just a single example from a QA dataset where key documents are positioned at the beginning. Wouldn’t it be more informative to compare the average contrastive perplexity of key documents against that of nonessential documents? Similarly, a comparison using standard perplexity might also be illuminating.

5. I believe there might be a typographical error: 'ground-true' should likely be corrected to "ground-truth."

reviews.summary: This paper proposes LongLLMLingua, a question-aware coarse-to-fine compression method to compress prompts and improve the key information density. The empirical results demonstrate that LongLLMLingua can substantially compress the prompts while maintaining the model performance and improving efficiency.
reviews.strengths:
1. The method is novel. This paper proposes to discard irrelevant documents and tokens iteratively in a coarse-to-fine manner, which is novel and effective. 
2. Measuring the token importance by perplexity is intuitive and insightful. Such an approach can be applied to black-box models, which is an advantage. 
3. The empirical results are sound, demonstrating the effectiveness and efficiency of LongLLMLingua.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. Prompt compression can be effective for tasks like QA, where the key information is sparsely distributed. In contrast to tasks, like summarization, the key information can be evenly distributed within inputs. Coarsely dropping a large amount of input may hurt the performance. 
2. The inference latency can be improved by LongLLMLingua. However, the models need to evaluate the perplexity and compress prompts every time, which leads to non-trivial latency.
reviews.questions:
1. How is the thresholding of $s_i$ determined to discard tokens of lower importance? Is it determined by the current budget $\tau_k$ of the documents? 
2. Section 4.3 introduces a dynamic budget scheduler. It is unclear to me how the iteration is defined here. Is there an iterative evaluation of token importance? 
3. I am interested in the perplexity distribution within sentences.  If the perplexity varies dramatically across tokens of one sentence, it can happen that a few tokens are retained sparsely, making the sentence inconsistent.

Summary:
This paper proposes LongLLMLingua, a question-aware coarse-to-fine compression method to compress prompts and improve the key information density. The empirical results demonstrate that LongLLMLingua can substantially compress the prompts while maintaining the model performance and improving efficiency.

Strengths:
1. The method is novel. This paper proposes to discard irrelevant documents and tokens iteratively in a coarse-to-fine manner, which is novel and effective. 
2. Measuring the token importance by perplexity is intuitive and insightful. Such an approach can be applied to black-box models, which is an advantage. 
3. The empirical results are sound, demonstrating the effectiveness and efficiency of LongLLMLingua.

Weaknesses:
1. Prompt compression can be effective for tasks like QA, where the key information is sparsely distributed. In contrast to tasks, like summarization, the key information can be evenly distributed within inputs. Coarsely dropping a large amount of input may hurt the performance. 
2. The inference latency can be improved by LongLLMLingua. However, the models need to evaluate the perplexity and compress prompts every time, which leads to non-trivial latency.

Questions:
1. How is the thresholding of $s_i$ determined to discard tokens of lower importance? Is it determined by the current budget $\tau_k$ of the documents? 
2. Section 4.3 introduces a dynamic budget scheduler. It is unclear to me how the iteration is defined here. Is there an iterative evaluation of token importance? 
3. I am interested in the perplexity distribution within sentences.  If the perplexity varies dramatically across tokens of one sentence, it can happen that a few tokens are retained sparsely, making the sentence inconsistent.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Expanded Review

Summary  
This paper proposes LongLLMLingua, a question-aware coarse-to-fine compression framework designed to accelerate inference and enhance performance of large language models (LLMs) in long-context scenarios. Specifically, LongLLMLingua comprises four key components: (1) a question-aware coarse-grained compression that ranks and prunes documents based on the conditional perplexity of the question (Eq. 2); (2) a document reordering mechanism that places the most relevant passages at the edges of the prompt to mitigate the “lost in the middle” effect (Eq. 4); (3) a dynamic compression ratio scheduler that allocates token budgets adaptively across documents according to their importance scores (Eq. 5); and (4) a post-compression subsequence recovery strategy (Algorithm 1) that restores entity-level integrity in the final responses. The authors evaluate LongLLMLingua on NaturalQuestions (multi-document QA with 20 retrieved passages), LongBench (16 tasks spanning summarization, code completion, few-shot learning, synthetic tasks, and QA), and ZeroSCROLLS (10 datasets covering summarization, QA, sentiment, and reordering). Experiments with GPT-3.5-Turbo-0613 and LongChat-13B-16k demonstrate that compressed prompts at 2×–10× reduction can yield up to 17.1% absolute accuracy improvement on NaturalQuestions (answer in 10th position) while reducing latency by up to 3.8× and saving tens of dollars per 1,000 queries.

Strengths  
1. Novel Coarse-to-Fine Pipeline  
   – The proposed integration of question-conditioned document ranking with iterative token-level pruning is original. By measuring rk via p(xquestr|xdock), the method discards irrelevant documents before applying contrastive perplexity-based token selection. This two-stage design goes beyond prior entropy-based compressors (Selective-Context, LLMLingua) and directly targets question relevance.  
   – The document reordering step (Section 4.2) is a clever yet simple addition: by sorting compressed documents by rk, the framework leverages LLMs’ stronger sensitivity to prefix/suffix information, as illustrated in Figure 1b. This addresses the middle-of-context degradation highlighted by Liu et al. (2023).  

2. Intuitive and Black-Box-Friendly Metrics  
   – Using perplexity and contrastive perplexity computed by a small surrogate model (LLaMA-2-7B-Chat) is conceptually straightforward and does not require access to LLM gradients or internals. This makes the approach immediately applicable to any black-box API-based service.  
   – The contrastive perplexity metric (Eq. 3) captures the difference between unconditional perplexity and question-conditioned perplexity, effectively highlighting tokens that contribute disproportionally to answering the query. Figure 3b shows that this metric clusters high-importance tokens in the ground-truth document.  

3. Thorough Empirical Validation  
   – The authors conduct ablations on NaturalQuestions (Table 3), LongBench (Table 6), and MuSiQue (Table 5), demonstrating that each component—question-aware coarse compression, fine-grained compression, dynamic ratios, reordering, and subsequence recovery—yields non-trivial gains.  
   – Cost analysis (Table 9) quantifies real-world savings, e.g., reducing GPT-3.5-Turbo inference costs on LongBench from \$31.5 to \$3.0 per 1,000 samples. Latency measurements (Table 1,2) on a V100 confirm up to 3.9× speedup.  

Weaknesses  
1. Task Sensitivity of Coarse Pruning  
   – While document- and token-level pruning excels in sparsely distributed-key scenarios like QA, its benefit on tasks with more uniformly distributed information—such as summarization or code completion—may be limited. In LongBench summarization, compression-based baselines saw only modest improvements or slight drops (Table 2). Coarsely dropping entire passages in such tasks could remove context needed for fluency or coherence, potentially harming performance.  
2. Latency Overhead of Compression Steps  
   – The framework reduces LLM API time but introduces overhead for surrogate-model inference on each document and token segment. Although end-to-end latency still decreases overall, the repeated computation of perplexities and contrastive perplexities every query is non-trivial. In low-resource or real-time settings, this upfront cost may negate some of the savings, especially at moderate compression rates.  

Questions  
1. Thresholding for Token Discarding  
   – How exactly is the cutoff for si determined within each segment? Is it solely governed by the dynamic budget τk (Eq. 5), or is there an additional margin or heuristic to guard against over-aggressive pruning?  
2. Iteration Definition in Dynamic Budget Scheduler  
   – Section 4.3 refers to an “iterative token-level compression algorithm” and a dynamic scheduler over iterations. Can the authors clarify what constitutes one iteration? Is the model recomputing token perplexities after each pruning pass, or are budgets adjusted only once per document?  
3. Intra-Sentence Perplexity Variance  
   – Have the authors observed cases where perplexity (or contrastive perplexity) varies dramatically within a single sentence? If so, how does the method prevent producing incoherent fragments when only a sparse subset of tokens from that sentence is retained? Is there any smoothing or minimum-span constraint to ensure contiguous sentence segments remain intact?  

Rating  
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper introduces a technique, LongLLMLingua, to accelerating and enhancing LLMs in Long-context via compression. The method involves several aspects, coarse-to-fine compression, reordering mechanism to reduce information loss in the middle. adaptive compression control and post-compression recovery strategy. Experiments are based upon GPT-3.5 api and LongChat-13B-16k models. It presents results on several benchmarks and show the effectiveness of the proposed method.
reviews.strengths:
1. The method is detailed and comprehensive. It includes many aspects that can be helpful for the compression and long-context prompting.

2. The compression ratio is great. It introduces 2x-10x compression rate and 1.4x-3.8x latency speed-up. It is very promising.

3. Results on benchmarks are good as shown in Table 1, 2, and 3.

4. The visualization in Figure 2 is very clear for me to understand this method.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. In the methods, it contains several aspects coarse compression, fine compression, reordering mechanism to reduce information loss in the middle. adaptive compression control and post-compression recovery strategy. It seems that it lacks a detailed ablation study that the influence on the compression rate and performance from each aspect. This is important for us to have a better understanding of this paper.

2. For the reordering method to avoid the lost-in-the-middle issue, I have a question that whether the reordering will disturb the time-line between each document. For example, if the input documents are some sections in a fiction, the latter sections depend on the content of the former ones to understand. If so, whether this operation will disturb or introduce other difficulties for understanding?

3. Based on Figure3b, it shows that contrastive perplexity is much more stable than the original perplexity metric. Would the authors provide more detailed explanation or mathematical proof on the reasons for this? It is a bit unclear for me to understand this difference.

4. In the benchmark comparison, it would be better to include the GPT-3.5-Turbo-0613 and LongChat-13B-16k for comparison. Although they might be slower and more expensive, it would be helpful to understand that the cost from the compression.
reviews.questions: Please see the weakness.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper introduces a technique, LongLLMLingua, to accelerating and enhancing LLMs in Long-context via compression. The method involves several aspects, coarse-to-fine compression, reordering mechanism to reduce information loss in the middle. adaptive compression control and post-compression recovery strategy. Experiments are based upon GPT-3.5 api and LongChat-13B-16k models. It presents results on several benchmarks and show the effectiveness of the proposed method.

Strengths:
1. The method is detailed and comprehensive. It includes many aspects that can be helpful for the compression and long-context prompting.

2. The compression ratio is great. It introduces 2x-10x compression rate and 1.4x-3.8x latency speed-up. It is very promising.

3. Results on benchmarks are good as shown in Table 1, 2, and 3.

4. The visualization in Figure 2 is very clear for me to understand this method.

Weaknesses:
1. In the methods, it contains several aspects coarse compression, fine compression, reordering mechanism to reduce information loss in the middle. adaptive compression control and post-compression recovery strategy. It seems that it lacks a detailed ablation study that the influence on the compression rate and performance from each aspect. This is important for us to have a better understanding of this paper.

2. For the reordering method to avoid the lost-in-the-middle issue, I have a question that whether the reordering will disturb the time-line between each document. For example, if the input documents are some sections in a fiction, the latter sections depend on the content of the former ones to understand. If so, whether this operation will disturb or introduce other difficulties for understanding?

3. Based on Figure3b, it shows that contrastive perplexity is much more stable than the original perplexity metric. Would the authors provide more detailed explanation or mathematical proof on the reasons for this? It is a bit unclear for me to understand this difference.

4. In the benchmark comparison, it would be better to include the GPT-3.5-Turbo-0613 and LongChat-13B-16k for comparison. Although they might be slower and more expensive, it would be helpful to understand that the cost from the compression.

Questions:
Please see the weakness.

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold
