reviews.text:
Review for “Rethinking Out-of-Distribution Detection on Imbalanced Data Distribution”

Original Decision (Rating): 6 – Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Summary  
This paper tackles the problem of out‐of‐distribution (OOD) detection under long‐tailed, imbalanced in‐distribution (ID) data. Through a series of empirical studies on CIFAR10‐LT, CIFAR100‐LT, and ImageNet‐LT, the authors demonstrate two pervasive failure modes of existing OOD detectors: (1) ID samples from tail classes are frequently misclassified as OOD, and (2) OOD samples are often assigned to head classes of the ID distribution. To explain these phenomena, they introduce ImOOD, a generalized statistical framework that extends the standard posterior model (P(y|x) ∝ P(x|y)P(y)) to open‐set scenarios under imbalanced priors. Their key theoretical insight identifies a class‐aware bias term, β(x) = ΣₙP(yₙ|x,i)/P(yₙ), which quantifies the gap between balanced and imbalanced detectors. Building on this analysis, they propose two remedies: a post‐hoc normalization (Eq. 9) that adds a log‐prior–weighted offset to the OOD logit, and a training‐time regularization (Eq. 10) that directly optimizes for the balanced ID/OOD posterior using learnable class priors. Experimentally, ImOOD substantially outperforms strong baselines (e.g. PASCL) by up to 5.2% AUROC on ImageNet‐LT, while improving tail‐class separability and reducing head‐class false positives in OOD detection.

Strengths  
1. Clear Motivation and Diagnostic Study: The paper begins with an insightful statistical analysis (Fig. 1) that empirically confirms both error modes (tail‐ID→OOD and OOD→head‐ID) across multiple detectors (OE, Energy, PASCL). This diagnostic motivates the development of a unified theory.  
2. Solid Theoretical Framework: The derivation in Section 3.1 is well‐structured, clearly showing how class priors introduce a bias term in P(i|x). The identification of β(x) and its impact on tail vs. head decisions is both intuitive and generalizable.  
3. Two‐Fold Solution: The post‐hoc normalization (Section 3.2) is a simple but effective plug‐in fix that only requires logging softmax outputs and priors. The training‐time regularizer (Section 3.3) builds on recent ideas in prompt‐style parameterization to learn class priors end‐to‐end, which empirically yields the best results.  
4. Strong Empirical Gains: On CIFAR10‐LT and CIFAR100‐LT, ImOOD improves AUROC by 1.7%–1.8% and reduces FPR95 by over 5 points compared to PASCL. On the more challenging ImageNet‐LT, it achieves a 5.2% AUROC gain and a 12.7% drop in FPR95. These consistent gains across three benchmarks underscore the practical value of the approach.  
5. Clarity and Reproducibility: The manuscript is well‐written, with a logical flow from problem diagnosis to theory to methods to experiments. Detailed implementation notes (Appendix A) and extensive ablations (Section 4.3, Appendix B) facilitate reproducibility.

Weaknesses and Questions  
1. Extension to Distance‐Based Methods  
   While ImOOD is demonstrated on post‐hoc confidence (OE, Energy, BinDisc), it remains unclear how the framework interacts with distance‐based OOD detectors such as Mahalanobis distance or K‐nearest neighbors in feature space. Could the class‐prior bias still be formulated in those settings? A brief discussion or experiment comparing ImOOD‐style normalization against state‐of‐the‐art distance‐based OOD (e.g. Ren et al.’s Mahalanobis approach) would strengthen the claims of generality.  

2. Performance on Synthesized Outliers  
   The paper evaluates ImOOD using real auxiliary OOD data (TinyImages80M, ImageNet‐Extra), but recent works (Tao et al. 2023) propose non‐parametric synthesized outliers when real outliers are unavailable. How does ImOOD perform with synthesized OOD samples? Does the learned prior πₙ(θ) remain stable if the auxiliary distribution is artificially generated rather than drawn from a large pool of natural images?  

3. Relation to Jiang et al. (2023)  
   Jiang et al. (ICML 2023) also leverage in‐distribution class prior for post‐hoc OOD calibration. It would be helpful if the authors elaborated on the precise connection and differentiation: for instance, Jiang et al. apply a fixed prior‐ratio adjustment to the MSP score on pre‐trained classifiers, whereas ImOOD derives a full Bayesian posterior adjustment and furthermore learns priors during training. A direct paragraph contrasting assumptions, formulations, and empirical gains would clarify how ImOOD advances beyond that very relevant baseline.  

Additional Suggestions  
– In Section 3.2, the authors propose taking log‐priors as an offset for stability, justified by Appendix A.3. It may be instructive to include a small ablation in the main text showing the effect of probability‐level vs. logit‐level calibration, to make the stability argument more tangible for readers.  
– Given that head and tail errors have different practical consequences, one might consider reporting separate AUROC curves for head‐classes vs. tail‐classes in the main paper, to more vividly show how β(x) influences each subpopulation.  
– In real deployments, data imbalance may evolve over time. A short discussion on how ImOOD could adapt to changing class distributions—e.g., by re‐estimating πₙ online—would provide practical guidance.

Overall, this paper offers a coherent theory and effective methods for OOD detection under long‐tailed data. Despite the above questions regarding distance‐based methods, synthesized outliers, and prior work, the contributions are substantial, and the empirical improvements are convincing. I recommend acceptance.

Rating  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

3. The results seem to outperform state-of-the-art.

Weaknesses:
1. I am curious about the possibility of this method being extended to distance-based OOD detection methods, such as Mahalanobios distance, etc. How well does the current method compare to the state-of-the-art distance-based methods?
2. I am curious about how well the current method performs on synthesized outliers (Tao et.al. 2023).
3. Could you elaborate more on a closely related baseline, Jiang et al. 2023, and discuss the similarities and differences w.r.t your work?

Questions:
see above

Rating:
6: marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The main contribution of this paper is the introduction of a generalized statistical framework called ImOOD, which addresses the problem of detecting and rejecting unknown out-of-distribution (OOD) samples in the presence of imbalanced data distributions. The paper identifies two common challenges faced by existing OOD detection methods: misclassifying tail class in-distribution (ID) samples as OOD, and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head class ID samples. Then, the authors propose a general framework that can lead to improved detection performance.
reviews.strengths:
The paper introduces a generalised statistical framework called ImOOD, which addresses the problem of OOD detection in the presence of imbalanced data distributions. Imbalanced data distributions widely exist in real world, and a set of fundamental works have proposed to tackle this questions to boost OOD detection. It seems that there may still exist many open questions, making the research direction in this paper promising in OOD detection. 

The paper identifies two common challenges faced by existing OOD detection methods, namely misclassifying tail class ID samples as OOD and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head class ID samples. This identification helps in understanding the limitations of current approaches.

The proposed method  is driven by the Bayesian analysis, demonstrating the impacts of data imbalance and suggesting a general framework that can handle the distribution shift in OOD detection. The proposed framework, as claimed by the authors, can be used to handle imbalanced issue for a set of different scoring strategies, and the evaluation results further verify the power of their method against imbalance data more or less.
reviews.weaknesses:
A direct question is that if we have used learning algorithms that can handle imbalanced data (which is often the case in reality) to train the basic model, do we need to handle imbalanced data for OOD detection thereafter. 

The authors identify two cases that make previous OOD detection methods fail, i.e., misclassifying tail class ID samples as OOD and incorrectly predicting OOD samples as head class ID samples. It is a direct conclusion and seemingly to be important, but could the authors further connect these two cases to previous works that handle imbalanced data in OOD detection and the proposed ImOOD. For example, why previous works, such as [1], will fail to discern these two cases and why ImOOD can overcome previous drawbacks. 

g should be defined in a proper position in advance. It seems that g should be the detector built upon f.

My main concern lies in the inaccurate estimation of beta, consisting of three terms that are all biased from my view. For P(i|X), a direct failure case is that when we have a detector whose g always greater than 0 (e.g., for distance based scoring), then P(i|X) will be always greater than 0.5. Thus, all data points will be taken as ID cases, making the estimation obviously a biased case. P(y|x,i) also suffers from biased estimation, due to the well known calibration failure of deep models. Such an issue, from my view, cannot be ignored in the field of OOD detection, since it is one of the main reason why MSP score is not effective in practice. The estimation of P(y) is also biased, since the training time data distribution (ID + OOD) is different from the test situation, thus n_o used for training cannot cover the real test situation. 


Detailed discussion about the hyper-parameters setting and the choice of auxiliary OOD data and the evaluation datasets should be discussed in detailed. More methods that handle OOD detection with imbalanced data should be considered here, such as [1]. More ablation studies to test the respective power of P(i|x), P(y), and P(y|x) should be tested.


[1] Detecting Out-of-distribution Data through In-distribution Class Prior.

title: Pure Message Passing Can Estimate Common Neighbor for Link Prediction

reviews.summary: Given that the representations of isomorphic nodes are the same, their link that aggregates the representations cannot capture some structural representation like CN, and thus it is intractable to perform the link prediction task. Based on this observation, this paper investigates the widely-used MPNN and finds that MPNN is capable of capturing joint structural features theoretically and empirically. The solution is to inject orthogonality into input vectors. Equipped with the orthogonality of the input vectors, the authors propose MPLP to estimate link information and accomplish the link prediction task. The experiments show that MPLP promotes significantly in the link prediction on both attributed/non-attributed datasets.
reviews.strengths:
+ This paper is a meaningful discussion of the previous work (Zhang et al., 2021) on whether GNNs/MPNNs can capture structural link representation. As I understand, the previous work believes that GNNs/MPNNs cannot do this supposing that isomorphic nodes have the same representation. So, this paper exploits the orthogonality in the input vectors, which is reasonable and does not totally contradict the previous work. This paper is basically valuable for supplementing the link prediction capability of GNNs/MPNNs in both theorems and experiments.
+ The authors propose a further discussion that some GNNs have the ability to perform link prediction tasks on attributed datasets due to the orthogonality of attributes. This is an interesting discussion that might contribute to the incense reason why GNNs/MPNNs can perform link prediction on attributed benchmarks. The empirical findings in Table 4 bring about the phenomenon and leave the issues that are worth discussing.
+ The results demonstrate that MPLP outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines on both attributed/non-attributed benchmarks.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The assumption of Gaussian distribution in the initialization of input vectors and weight matrices is reasonable for non-attributed benchmarks but it seems not to be correspondent for attributed benchmarks.
- The research focuses on the capability of MPNN for estimating common neighbors which is a critical heuristic for link prediction. In other words, MPLP injects the CN heuristic into MPNN. So, what about the link prediction performance when we directly regard CN as the node attribute? For example, the first elements of the input vectors could be substituted with CN value.
reviews.questions:
- Why are the results of SAGE on some benchmarks (Computers/Photo) different between Table 2 and Table 4?
- I am curious about when we concatenate the attribute vector with random feats, would the results be better than those with only attributes or only random feats? Since it is hard to evaluate whether attribute vectors are more important than random feats before conducting the experiments, the concatenation might achieve the better one automatically.
- What does the author mean by `MPLP holds its own’? What proves that?
- What is the potential impact when you find MPNN can promote link prediction? In what cases or scenarios will MPLP be used and benefit the industry?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
Given that the representations of isomorphic nodes are the same, their link that aggregates the representations cannot capture some structural representation like CN, and thus it is intractable to perform the link prediction task. Based on this observation, this paper investigates the widely-used MPNN and finds that MPNN is capable of capturing joint structural features theoretically and empirically. The solution is to inject orthogonality into input vectors. Equipped with the orthogonality of the input vectors, the authors propose MPLP to estimate link information and accomplish the link prediction task. The experiments show that MPLP promotes significantly in the link prediction on both attributed/non-attributed datasets.

Strengths:
+ This paper is a meaningful discussion of the previous work (Zhang et al., 2021) on whether GNNs/MPNNs can capture structural link representation. As I understand, the previous work believes that GNNs/MPNNs cannot do this supposing that isomorphic nodes have the same representation. So, this paper exploits the orthogonality in the input vectors, which is reasonable and does not totally contradict the previous work. This paper is basically valuable for supplementing the link prediction capability of GNNs/MPNNs in both theorems and experiments.
+ The authors propose a further discussion that some GNNs have the ability to perform link prediction tasks on attributed datasets due to the orthogonality of attributes. This is an interesting discussion that might contribute to the incense reason why GNNs/MPNNs can perform link prediction on attributed benchmarks. The empirical findings in Table 4 bring about the phenomenon and leave the issues that are worth discussing.
+ The results demonstrate that MPLP outperforms the state-of-the-art baselines on both attributed/non-attributed benchmarks.

Weaknesses:
- The assumption of Gaussian distribution in the initialization of input vectors and weight matrices is reasonable for non-attributed benchmarks but it seems not to be correspondent for attributed benchmarks.
- The research focuses on the capability of MPNN for estimating common neighbors which is a critical heuristic for link prediction. In other words, MPLP injects the CN heuristic into MPNN. So, what about the link prediction performance when we directly regard CN as the node attribute? For example, the first elements of the input vectors could be substituted with CN value.

Questions:
- Why are the results of SAGE on some benchmarks (Computers/Photo) different between Table 2 and Table 4?
- I am curious about when we concatenate the attribute vector with random feats, would the results be better than those with only attributes or only random feats? Since it is hard to evaluate whether attribute vectors are more important than random feats before conducting the experiments, the concatenation might achieve the better one automatically.
- What does the author mean by `MPLP holds its own’? What proves that?
- What is the potential impact when you find MPNN can promote link prediction? In what cases or scenarios will MPLP be used and benefit the industry?

Rating:
8: accept, good paper
reviews.text:
Summary:
The manuscript under review addresses a core limitation of Message Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) for link prediction, namely their inability—when initialized with identical or permutation-invariant node features—to distinguish isomorphic nodes and thereby capture joint structural heuristics such as Common Neighbors (CN). Building upon the observation of Zhang et al. (2021) that GNNs/MPNNs with standard initialization cannot represent link-level overlap features, the authors rigorously investigate both theoretically and empirically how injecting orthogonality into node input vectors overcomes this barrier. They demonstrate via Theorem 1 and Theorem 2 that, with high-dimensional quasi-orthogonal (QO) vectors, a pure message-passing scheme can unbiasedly estimate CN (and even weighted heuristics like Adamic–Adar and Resource Allocation). Based on these insights, they propose the Message Passing Link Predictor (MPLP), which (i) samples node signatures from a unit hypercube to guarantee QO properties, (ii) employs “one-hot hub” vectors for high-degree nodes to reduce estimation variance, (iii) rescales vector norms via an MLP to capture weighted neighbor counts, and (iv) constructs structural features—distance-encoding counts and triangle counts—solely via message passing. Empirical evaluations on eight non-attributed and five attributed benchmarks (including USAir, Yeast, C.elegans, CS, Photo, Collab) show that MPLP outperforms thirteen strong baselines—heuristic-only, node-level (GCN, GraphSAGE) and link-level (SEAL, Neo-GNN, ELPH, NCNC)—by 2–10% in Hits@50, and consistently ranks among the top performers under Hits@20 and Hits@100. The authors also provide comprehensive ablation studies (Appendix D) to validate the contributions of shortcut removal, one-hot hubs, norm rescaling, choice of r-hop features, batch size, and vector dimension.

Strengths:
+ **Novel theoretical insight**: Whereas prior work (Zhang et al., 2021; Chen et al., 2020) ruled out GNNs from capturing link-level overlap purely via permutation-invariant node features, this manuscript overturns that assumption by showing that QO input vectors restore the ability to count shared neighbors. The proof of Theorem 2, which gives an unbiased estimator for CN(u,v) with variance \(1/F\,\bigl[d_ud_v + \mathrm{CN}(u,v)^2 - 2\,\mathrm{CN}(u,v)+F\Var(x^2)\,\mathrm{CN}(u,v)\bigr]\), is particularly elegant and leans on the same random-projection arguments as Johnson–Lindenstrauss.
+ **Comprehensive method design**: The authors do more than inject random vectors. They (i) reserve a subspace for one-hot hubs to curb variance in power-law graphs, (ii) propose MLP-driven norm rescaling (Equation 4) to approximate RA and AA heuristics, (iii) implement distance-encoded counts \(\#(p,q)\) via targeted message passing (Equation 5–6), and (iv) approximate triangle counts (Equation 7) using two- and three-hop walks. This pure message-passing solution sidesteps expensive subgraph extraction (as in SEAL) yet achieves similar expressiveness.
+ **Strong empirical validation**: Table 1 and Table 2 show that MPLP leads on 12 out of 13 benchmarks in Hits@50. Figure 4 demonstrates that even a “no-feat” variant of MPLP (260 parameters) rivals models with orders of magnitude more parameters, and has competitive inference time. Figures 5 and 8 confirm that MPLP estimates \(\#(1,1),\#(1,2),\#(2,2)\), etc., with lower MSE than ELPH’s MinHash/HyperLogLog below 3000 dimensions, and that one-hot hubs indeed reduce estimation variance. Answers to the expressiveness gap claim in Wang et al. (2023) are provided by the substructure-counting ablations in Table 7–8.
+ **Insightful discussion of attributed graphs**: Section 3.2 and Appendix C analyze the fact that bag-of-words or embedding-based attributes often form a quasi-orthogonal basis themselves. Table 4’s experiments replacing real attributes with random input features (matched in dimension) reveal that on some datasets (Computers, Photo) random QO input performs as well or better—highlighting that structural overlap, not only raw node content, drives link prediction performance in these benchmarks.

Weaknesses / Suggestions for Improvement:
- The assumption of Gaussian initialization for node and weight matrices (Theorem 1) is well-justified for non-attributed graphs with synthetic QO vectors, but its direct correspondence to real attributes in attributed benchmarks (CS, Photo, Collab) is less clear. A brief discussion on how real attributes deviate from zero-mean Gaussian assumptions, and whether any centering or normalization step is needed in practice, would strengthen the treatment.
- Since MPLP explicitly builds on the CN heuristic, one might ask: what if the raw CN count were directly used as a scalar node attribute or concatenated to existing features? Would a simple feature augmentation (node features ← [original features; CN values]) match or exceed MPLP’s performance? An empirical side-by-side comparison could clarify the relative value of full QO vector propagation versus a scalar feature injection.
- In the practical deployment of MPLP—e.g., real-time recommender systems or protein-interaction screening—the training overhead incurred by repeated shortcut removal (Section 4.2) could be significant. A discussion of strategies to amortize or approximate this step—perhaps by masking only a subset of positive edges per batch or by precomputing r-hop neighborhoods on a static “training graph”—would be valuable for industry adoption.
- The paper reports that GraphSAGE sometimes outperforms in estimating RA (Figure 2) but lags on CN/AA; yet Table 4 shows divergent SAGE results on Computers/Photo between the two experiment sets. Clarifying whether these discrepancies arise from different feature initializations (Gaussian vs. random ±1), layer depths, or hyperparameter settings would resolve reader confusion.

Questions / Clarifications:
- The authors observe in Table 4 that MPLP with random-feat input sometimes surpasses the same GNN with real attributes. Have the authors tried concatenating real attributes with random QO signatures? Would this hybrid yield further gains, as it might allow the model to leverage both raw content and structural proxies?
- The manuscript mentions in Section 5 that MPLP “holds its own” against node-level baselines. Could the authors elaborate on which metrics or error bars justify this phrase? Is it based on matched performance on E.coli or runtime comparisons in Figure 4?
- Beyond credits on standard academic benchmarks, in what practical scenarios—social network growth prediction, molecular interaction screening, knowledge-graph completion—would MPLP be particularly well-suited? A brief case study or illustrative example would help anchor the method’s industrial impact.

Conclusion and Recommendation:
Overall, this work makes a significant and well-substantiated advance in the theory and practice of link prediction with GNNs. By showing that a pure message-passing scheme, when combined with high-dimensional random or quasi-orthogonal node signatures, can unbiasedly estimate core structural heuristics (CN, AA, RA, subgraph counts) and deliver state-of-the-art performance with efficient inference, the authors close an important gap in GNN expressiveness. The balance of rigorous theorems, thoughtful method construction, and extensive empirical validation justifies an acceptance decision. I recommend acceptance (Rating: 8: good paper).

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors explore the use of pure Message-Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) for link prediction in graphs. The paper starts by exploring the known limitations of MPNNs related to their permutation invariance property for link prediction and introduces an approach called Message Passing Link Predictor (MPLP) that leverages a node "signature". This feature vector essentially consists of a one-hot quasi-orthogonal vector, which the authors claim as a solution for MPNNs and more accurate link prediction. Experimental validation on 13 different graph datasets, both attributed and non-attributed is performed in order to concretely validate the claims made in the paper.
reviews.strengths:
- Originality: Pure Message-Passing for Link Prediction is not as well-understood as it should be, so the idea of enhancing GNNs to better handle the estimation of heuristic methods is a strong avenue for generating original work. MPLP does a fine job of this through it's problem formulation, equations, and theorems. It also takes care of tying in their motivations to numerous other relevant papers. 
- Quality: The spread of datasets captures a variety of domains and the experiments are structured in a ways that directly supports the author's claims.
- Clarity: The principles for the theorems and experiments are well-founded, it respects the reader's background in understanding related GNN works and keeps the discussion about any innovation presented in the paper succinct.
- Significance: The use of quasi-orthogonal vectors as a means of enhancing the message-passing capabilities of GNNs is entirely novel as far as I know, albeit it does extend principles from SOTA models. Future research could be inspired by a high-level approach that is similar to MPLP. MPLP's ability to estimate CN and DE is also a promising and significant improvement for GNN's ability to conduct link prediction. The non-attributed benchmark test, as shown in Table 1, is an interesting inclusion that speaks to the power of MPLP to handle non-standard link prediction scenarios. The inference experiments are thorough, considering the estimation of multple labels in regard to signatue dimension, inference, and ablation studies on batch-size.
reviews.weaknesses:
- The link prediction results for attributed benchmarks, as shown in Table 2 is limited in that it does not include results from all of the standard datasets: ogbl-ppa, ogbl-ddi, ogbl-citation2. The OGBL datasets are included as baselines in all of the included SOTA models, the results from which would serve as a direct comparison for MPLP's performance versus any SOTA method. 
- The first concern is compounded in that it is difficult to truly tell how well MPLP improves estimation of labels given that it only considers ogbl-collab versus ELPH and not the more scalable BUDDY. 
- The experiments, as shown in Table 1 and 2 only consider results for Hits@50. This seems relevant for the subsequent evaluations of inference, dimensions, and batch sizes. But, is still limited since the current SOTA models run experiments with Hits@100, Hits@20, and MRR.
- The test for the estimation capabilities seems limited by testing just GCN and SAGE. The inner product calculation seems like it should be extended to other types of popular GNNs such as: GIN and GAT. The results from which would lend more credence to the claims made about expressiveness and the effects of implementing quasi-orthogonal vectors into link prediction models. 
- The node-label estimation of CN and DE is promising. However, it seems limited in scope since it approximates just CN or DE and does not extend further to DRNL or DE+. This may be due to concerns of tractable computation but given SEAL's explicit testing of both DRNL and DE++ as labelling tricks, this seems like an important inclusion to evaluate MPLP fully.
reviews.questions:
- What was the reasoning behind not including the remaining OGBL datasets? It seems that MPLP's scalability is an important component based on the inference, dimensions, and ablation studies but why not bring the scalability to the forefront of the paper instead of placing the ablation studies in the appendix.
- The extent that GAT could be effectively tested against MPLP seems limited given that GAT relies on attention and MPLP does not have explicitly have a mechanism to consider this. However, when considering the expressiveness of MPLP with random features and it's ability to estimate triangles, why not include a provably expressive GNN to test like GIN?
- What sort of limitations would integrating DRNL into GCN or SAGE pose? Is it concern over tractability and the fact that in certain instances DRNL is similar to DE and CN?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

- Quality: The spread of datasets captures a variety of domains and the experiments are structured in a ways that directly supports the author's claims.
- Clarity: The principles for the theorems and experiments are well-founded, it respects the reader's background in understanding related GNN works and keeps the discussion about any innovation presented in the paper succinct.
- Significance: The use of quasi-orthogonal vectors as a means of enhancing the message-passing capabilities of GNNs is entirely novel as far as I know, albeit it does extend principles from SOTA models. Future research could be inspired by a high-level approach that is similar to MPLP. MPLP's ability to estimate CN and DE is also a promising and significant improvement for GNN's ability to conduct link prediction. The non-attributed benchmark test, as shown in Table 1, is an interesting inclusion that speaks to the power of MPLP to handle non-standard link prediction scenarios. The inference experiments are thorough, considering the estimation of multple labels in regard to signatue dimension, inference, and ablation studies on batch-size.

Weaknesses:
- The link prediction results for attributed benchmarks, as shown in Table 2 is limited in that it does not include results from all of the standard datasets: ogbl-ppa, ogbl-ddi, ogbl-citation2. The OGBL datasets are included as baselines in all of the included SOTA models, the results from which would serve as a direct comparison for MPLP's performance versus any SOTA method. 
- The first concern is compounded in that it is difficult to truly tell how well MPLP improves estimation of labels given that it only considers ogbl-collab versus ELPH and not the more scalable BUDDY. 
- The experiments, as shown in Table 1 and 2 only consider results for Hits@50. This seems relevant for the subsequent evaluations of inference, dimensions, and batch sizes. But, is still limited since the current SOTA models run experiments with Hits@100, Hits@20, and MRR.
- The test for the estimation capabilities seems limited by testing just GCN and SAGE. The inner product calculation seems like it should be extended to other types of popular GNNs such as: GIN and GAT. The results from which would lend more credence to the claims made about expressiveness and the effects of implementing quasi-orthogonal vectors into link prediction models. 
- The node-label estimation of CN and DE is promising. However, it seems limited in scope since it approximates just CN or DE and does not extend further to DRNL or DE+. This may be due to concerns of tractable computation but given SEAL's explicit testing of both DRNL and DE++ as labelling tricks, this seems like an important inclusion to evaluate MPLP fully.

Questions:
- What was the reasoning behind not including the remaining OGBL datasets? It seems that MPLP's scalability is an important component based on the inference, dimensions, and ablation studies but why not bring the scalability to the forefront of the paper instead of placing the ablation studies in the appendix.
- The extent that GAT could be effectively tested against MPLP seems limited given that GAT relies on attention and MPLP does not have explicitly have a mechanism to consider this. However, when considering the expressiveness of MPLP with random features and it's ability to estimate triangles, why not include a provably expressive GNN to test like GIN?
- What sort of limitations would integrating DRNL into GCN or SAGE pose? Is it concern over tractability and the fact that in certain instances DRNL is similar to DE and CN?

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

Summary:
In this paper, the authors explore the use of pure Message-Passing Neural Networks (MPNNs) for link prediction in graphs. The paper starts by exploring the known limitations of MPNNs related to their permutation invariance property for link prediction and introduces an approach called Message Passing Link Predictor (MPLP) that leverages a node "signature". This feature vector essentially consists of a one-hot quasi-orthogonal vector, which the authors claim as a solution for MPNNs and more accurate link prediction. Experimental validation on 13 different graph datasets, both attributed and non-attributed is performed in order to concretely validate the claims made in the paper.

Strengths:
- Originality: Pure Message-Passing for Link Prediction is not as well-understood as it should be, so the idea of enhancing GNNs to better handle the estimation of heuristic methods is a strong avenue for generating original work. MPLP does a fine job of this through it's problem formulation, equations, and theorems. It also takes care of tying in their motivations to numerous other relevant papers. 
- Quality: The spread of datasets captures a variety of domains and the experiments are structured in a ways that directly supports the author's claims.
- Clarity: The principles for the theorems and experiments are well-founded, it respects the reader's background in understanding related GNN works and keeps the discussion about any innovation presented in the paper succinct.
- Significance: The use of quasi-orthogonal vectors as a means of enhancing the message-passing capabilities of GNNs is entirely novel as far as I know, albeit it does extend principles from SOTA models. Future research could be inspired by a high-level approach that is similar to MPLP. MPLP's ability to estimate CN and DE is also a promising and significant improvement for GNN's ability to conduct link prediction. The non-attributed benchmark test, as shown in Table 1, is an interesting inclusion that speaks to the power of MPLP to handle non-standard link prediction scenarios. The inference experiments are thorough, considering the estimation of multple labels in regard to signatue dimension, inference, and ablation studies on batch-size.

Weaknesses:
- The link prediction results for attributed benchmarks, as shown in Table 2 is limited in that it does not include results from all of the standard datasets: ogbl-ppa, ogbl-ddi, ogbl-citation2. The OGBL datasets are included as baselines in all of the included SOTA models, the results from which would serve as a direct comparison for MPLP's performance versus any SOTA method. 
- The first concern is compounded in that it is difficult to truly tell how well MPLP improves estimation of labels given that it only considers ogbl-collab versus ELPH and not the more scalable BUDDY. 
- The experiments, as shown in Table 1 and 2 only consider results for Hits@50. This seems relevant for the subsequent evaluations of inference, dimensions, and batch sizes. But, is still limited since the current SOTA models run experiments with Hits@100, Hits@20, and MRR.
- The test for the estimation capabilities seems limited by testing just GCN and SAGE. The inner product calculation seems like it should be extended to other types of popular GNNs such as: GIN and GAT. The results from which would lend more credence to the claims made about expressiveness and the effects of implementing quasi-orthogonal vectors into link prediction models. 
- The node-label estimation of CN and DE is promising. However, it seems limited in scope since it approximates just CN or DE and does not extend further to DRNL or DE+. This may be due to concerns of tractable computation but given SEAL's explicit testing of both DRNL and DE++ as labelling tricks, this seems like an important inclusion to evaluate MPLP fully.

- What was the reasoning behind not including the remaining OGBL datasets? It seems that MPLP's scalability is an important component based on the inference, dimensions, and ablation studies but why not bring the scalability to the forefront of the paper instead of placing the ablation studies in the appendix.

reviews.summary: In this paper, the authors propose that by harnessing the orthogonality of input vectors, pure message-passing can capture the common neighbor heuristics. This idea is very straightforward. My main concern is that the ablation study (as evaluated in Table 5) shows that the main working component is the shortcut removal. Also, the main results reported in Tables 1 and 2 show that the new method only brings marginal improvements. Overall, I give a weak rejection.
reviews.strengths:
1. It is an interesting topic to study the connection between GNNs and common neighbor heuristics.
2. It is great to see the theoretical analysis of GNNs on capturing the common neighbor heuristics.
3. The authors have a very comprehensive experiment for the evaluation of the proposed method.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. The results show that the improvements of the new method in many cases are marginal. 
2. The paper writing of this paper needs to be heavily improved.
3. The ablation study shows that some of the proposed components do not work well.
reviews.questions: It is common sense that in many link prediction datasets, the common neighbor heuristics can outperform GCNs. Therefore, I think it is very interesting and important to study the connection between the message passing and the common neighbor heuristics. It is straightforward to establish a one-hot vector to encode the neighbor node information. I like the theoretical analysis in Theorem 2. However, I do not get how you derive the CN from the product operation and use them in the GNNs. I am trying to understand Figure 3, but there is no caption explaining what colors stand for. Also, in Theorem 3, you introduce a walk operation counting the number of length-l walks between nodes u and node v. I wonder how you conduct this operation in practice. I suspect this operation would be super time-consuming to enumerate all the possible walks. For the experiment section, I consider your method as a general method that can be applied to various GNNs. So, I expect the authors to compare their method with the baselines under the same GNN base such as GCN or SAGE. Another main issue is that the proposed method only gets marginal improvement (not significant improvement according to the mean and the std) compared to the baselines in many cases. Therefore, overall, I would like to give a weak rejection, but if the authors can answer my above questions or point out my misunderstanding part, I will be happy to raise my score.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this paper, the authors propose that by harnessing the orthogonality of input vectors, pure message-passing can capture the common neighbor heuristics. This idea is very straightforward. My main concern is that the ablation study (as evaluated in Table 5) shows that the main working component is the shortcut removal. Also, the main results reported in Tables 1 and 2 show that the new method only brings marginal improvements. Overall, I give a weak rejection.

Strengths:
1. It is an interesting topic to study the connection between GNNs and common neighbor heuristics.
2. It is great to see the theoretical analysis of GNNs on capturing the common neighbor heuristics.
3. The authors have a very comprehensive experiment for the evaluation of the proposed method.

Weaknesses:
1. The results show that the improvements of the new method in many cases are marginal. 
2. The paper writing of this paper needs to be heavily improved.
3. The ablation study shows that some of the proposed components do not work well.

Questions:
It is common sense that in many link prediction datasets, the common neighbor heuristics can outperform GCNs. Therefore, I think it is very interesting and important to study the connection between the message passing and the common neighbor heuristics. It is straightforward to establish a one-hot vector to encode the neighbor node information. I like the theoretical analysis in Theorem 2. However, I do not get how you derive the CN from the product operation and use them in the GNNs. I am trying to understand Figure 3, but there is no caption explaining what colors stand for. Also, in Theorem 3, you introduce a walk operation counting the number of length-l walks between nodes u and node v. I wonder how you conduct this operation in practice. I suspect this operation would be super time-consuming to enumerate all the possible walks. For the experiment section, I consider your method as a general method that can be applied to various GNNs. So, I expect the authors to compare their method with the baselines under the same GNN base such as GCN or SAGE. Another main issue is that the proposed method only gets marginal improvement (not significant improvement according to the mean and the std) compared to the baselines in many cases. Therefore, overall, I would like to give a weak rejection, but if the authors can answer my above questions or point out my misunderstanding part, I will be happy to raise my score.

5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper proposes to assign each node with a quasi-orthogonal (QO) vector as its id (signature) and then run pure message-passing over them, so that the obtained node embeddings can be used to estimate link-level structural features (e.g. common neighbors), which is unattainable by vanilla GNNs. A new framework based on estimated pairwise features is proposed for the link prediction task, whose effectiveness is evaluated on 13 non-attributed and attributed graphs.
reviews.strengths:
- Similarly to subgraph sketching, the author proposes an estimation-based approach to obtain pairwise structural features of link prediction heuristics under the message-passing framework. 
- The authors exploit the property of quasi-orthogonal (QO) vectors so that neighbor overlap-based heuristics other than CN can be estimated by the simplified MPNN framework.
- The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
reviews.weaknesses:
- In order to estimate pairwise features, the pure message passing adopted in MPLP loses the non-linearity of MPNN, which comprises the expressiveness of the framework.
- The adopted QO vector from DotHash enables more types of pairwise structural features (AA, RA), but it is still heuristic-based. Those features are empirically helpful for link prediction but also lose flexibility and capacity since they are fixed and predefined.
- The feature estimation of Eq. (5) still depends on the $r$-hop induced subgraph for estimating $r$-order intersection and difference of neighborhoods. Meanwhile, Theorem 3 shows that $\mathbf{h}_u^{r}$ and $\mathbf{h}_v^{r}$ can not be used for CN. This raises my concern over its scalability (feature propgation+subgraph extraction), and it would be great to see a clearer side-by-side runtime comparison with BUDDY [1], including preprocessing (node-wise) and estimating structural features (link-wise).

[1] Chamberlain, Benjamin Paul, et al. "Graph neural networks for link prediction with subgraph sketching." ICLR (2023).
reviews.questions:
- How would the sampling of neighborhoods in SAGE affect Thereom 1 and the estimator in Eq. (3)?
- What is the cost of generating QO vectors? Is there a principal way to pick its dimension? Will it scale to graphs larger than collab? How would the density of the graph affect its performance of runtime and estimation error?
- The order of features $r$ used in experiments is 2. Is it based on computation concerns? Can MPLP be applied beyond 2 hops?
- Sec 4.3 mentioned that Eq. (7) can be used for estimating triangles. Can MPLP be used for the triangle estimation task in Sec 6.1 of [2]?
- Is the $\text{GNN}(\cdot)$ in Eq. (4) and Eq. (8) referring to valina GNNs or the linearized version proposed in Eq. (3)?
- It would be great to provide more details regarding the baselines, especially differentiating ELPH and BUDDY [1], and NCN/NCNC-k [3] for interpreting the results of Table 2 and Fig 4.
- It is a standard procedure to remove target links in training for inductive settings. Similar approaches have been adopted in SEAL [4] and SUREL (mini-batch subgraph training) [5].

[2] Chen, Zhengdao, et al. "Can graph neural networks count substructures?." Advances in neural information processing systems 33 (2020): 10383-10395.   
[3] Wang, Xiyuan, Haotong Yang, and Muhan Zhang. "Neural Common Neighbor with Completion for Link Prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2302.00890 (2023).  
[4] Zhang, Muhan, and Yixin Chen. "Link prediction based on graph neural networks." Advances in neural information processing systems 31 (2018).  
[5] Yin, Haoteng, et al. "Algorithm and system co-design for efficient subgraph-based graph representation learning." VLDB (2022).

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper presents a novel link prediction framework, MPLP, that assigns each node a quasi-orthogonal (QO) vector signature and applies pure message-passing to these vectors to estimate pairwise structural features—such as Common Neighbors (CN), Adamic–Adar (AA), Resource Allocation (RA), and even triangle counts—that are known to be challenging for standard GNNs due to permutation invariance. By leveraging the inner products of propagated QO node signatures, MPLP constructs estimators for these heuristics, integrates them (along with optional node attribute encodings via a vanilla GNN) into a link-level feature vector, and then classifies link existence. The method is evaluated across 13 benchmarks (8 non-attributed, 5 attributed) and uniformly surpasses classical heuristics (CN, AA, RA), node-level GNNs (GCN, SAGE), and several link-level baselines (SEAL, Neo-GNN, ELPH, NCNC) in Hits@50, Hits@20, and Hits@100 while maintaining competitive inference speed.

Strengths:
- The core idea of using QO vectors under pure message-passing to estimate pairwise structural features parallels subgraph sketching, but remains strictly within the MPNN formalism, making it a conceptually elegant extension.
- Beyond counting CN, the paper shows (both theoretically in Theorems 1–3 and empirically in Figure 2) that by carefully choosing the aggregation scheme and input vector distributions, simpler MPNNs (GCN, SAGE) can in fact approximate AA and RA. MPLP leverages this insight to recover multiple heuristic counts within one unified framework.
- The treatment of triangle estimation (Equation 7, Section 4.3) is particularly notable: despite prior impossibility results, the authors demonstrate how second-order message-passing can approximate triangle counts attached to a target link, expanding the expressiveness of pure message-passing.
- The manuscript is well-written, logically organized, and provides thorough proofs (Appendix E), detailed experimental protocols (Appendix B), and ablations that dissect each component’s utility (Appendix D).

Weaknesses:
- By restricting MPLP to pure message-passing (Section 3.2, Equation 3), the framework necessarily drops the non-linear MLP updates present in standard MPNNs. While this bias reduction yields unbiased CN estimates (Theorem 2), it also removes the flexibility and expressiveness gained from non-linear transformations, potentially limiting learning capacity when heuristic counts are insufficient.
- Although QO vectors from DotHash enable MPLP to estimate AA and RA in addition to CN, these features remain heuristic-based and fixed ahead of time. This contrasts with fully learned representations, and it may hamper the model’s adaptability on graphs where emergent structural patterns differ markedly from the chosen heuristics.
- The procedure in Equation (5) for enumerating Distance Encoding (DE) counts still requires extracting an r-hop induced subgraph per target link and then propagating signatures over it. Coupled with the need to remove shortcut edges during training (Section 4.2), this raises practical concerns about scalability: feature propagation plus per-link subgraph extraction can become expensive on large or dense graphs.
- While MPLP’s inference cost is relatively low (see Figure 4 on Collab: MPLP completes a full epoch ~0.5 s compared to ~0.7 s for ELPH and ~4 s for SEAL), the training overhead is not reported. Since shortcut removal forces recomputation of shortest-path neighborhoods per batch, the training time and memory footprint may be substantial—particularly when batch sizes are small and subgraph extraction is repeated. A clear side-by-side runtime and memory comparison with BUDDY [1], including both node-level preprocessing and link-wise feature estimation, would strengthen the practical claims.
  
Questions and Suggestions:
1. Theorem 1 hinges on full neighbor aggregation in SAGE. In practice, SAGE often uses neighborhood sampling for scalability. How would subsampling of neighbors impact the unbiasedness or bias of the proposed estimators in Theorem 1 and Equation (3)? Would the variance grow, and is there a principled correction?
2. Generating QO vectors requires sampling from a high-dimensional hypercube (Section 4.1). Can the authors clarify the computational cost of this process, and recommend how to choose the vector dimension F in relation to graph size n and edge density? Specifically, will the method scale to graphs substantially larger than Collab (n ≈ 23 k), and how does density (average degree) affect both runtime and estimation accuracy?
3. The experiments fix the neighborhood radius at r = 2 hops. Is this choice driven purely by computational trade-offs? Could MPLP be extended to r > 2? If so, how do the counts of walks (Theorem 3) and induced subgraph extraction scale with r, and is there a point of diminishing returns?
4. Equation (7) proposes a triangle estimator for each endpoint. Can MPLP be directly applied to the standalone triangle counting task described in Section 6.1 of Chen et al. [2]? If so, how does it compare to specialized substructure-counting GNNs or classical triangle counting algorithms?
5. In Equations (4) and (8), GNN(·) denotes an attribute encoder. Do the authors use a linearized version (pure sum aggregation, no MLP) as in Equation (3), or a full vanilla GNN with non-linear updates? Clarifying this will aid reproducibility.
6. The baseline descriptions could be expanded. In particular, how do ELPH and BUDDY [1] differ in preprocessing and estimation? Similarly, NCN vs. NCNC-k [3] use distinct completion strategies. A brief side-by-side table summarizing preprocessing time, feature dimension, and inference complexity would help interpret Table 2 and Figure 4.
7. The practice of removing target links during training to prevent shortcut edges is standard in inductive link prediction (SEAL [4], SUREL [5]). A concise acknowledgment of these precedents, and a discussion on how MPLP’s version differs (e.g., batched removal vs. full graph removal), would affirm that shortcut removal is not novel but a best practice for fair evaluation.

Decision:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

References:
[1] Chamberlain, B. P., et al. “Graph Neural Networks for Link Prediction with Subgraph Sketching.” ICLR 2023.  
[2] Chen, Z., et al. “Can Graph Neural Networks Count Substructures?” NeurIPS 2020.  
[3] Wang, X., Yang, H., Zhang, M. “Neural Common Neighbor with Completion for Link Prediction.” arXiv 2023.  
[4] Zhang, M., Chen, Y. “Link Prediction Based on Graph Neural Networks.” NeurIPS 2018.  
[5] Yin, H., et al. “Algorithm and System Co-Design for Efficient Subgraph-based Graph Representation Learning.” VLDB 2022.

title: RINGER: Conformer Ensemble Generation of Macrocyclic Peptides with Sequence-Conditioned Internal Coordinate Diffusion

reviews.summary:
This paper addresses the challenge of generating macrocyclic peptides. The approach involves training a discrete-time diffusion model on the internal coordinates, which is implemented using a transformer equipped with its specific invariant cyclic positional encoding tailored for this generation task. During inference, the set of angles and torsions generated undergoes a refinement phase through constrained optimization.

The primary contribution of this work is the innovative architectural design tailored to tackle conformer generation for this particular class of molecules. Additionally, the paper provides comprehensive experimental evidence to establish the suitability of their approach for the problem.
reviews.strengths:
Significance:
Macrocyclic peptides represent a crucial category in therapeutics, and enhancing the precision and efficiency of conformer generation can profoundly impact drug discovery. Thus, this paper addresses a highly significant problem in the field.

Originality:
The paper introduces two key technical innovations. First, it adapts the positional encoding of the transformer architecture to better suit cyclic peptides, showcasing the authors' domain-specific knowledge in their modeling approach. Second, the paper presents a straightforward yet effective ring-closing procedure based on constraint optimization. Both solutions highlight that the authors intelligently use their task-specific insights. Moreover, the paper goes beyond traditional metrics for conformer generation, introducing novel evaluation criteria better aligned with the task.

Clarity and Quality:
The text is well-crafted, effectively motivating the problem, and the literature review is well-structured and relevant to the context.
reviews.weaknesses:
An ablation study is essential to elucidate the respective contributions of the positional encoder and the ring closing algorithm to the overall performance.

The benchmarking falls short in including some of the most recent diffusion models for small molecule conformer generation. While the authors acknowledge their limitations, substantiating these claims with experimental evidence is crucial. It would be valuable to include models such as TorsionDiff and a non-diffusion model like GFlowNets for a more comprehensive evaluation.
reviews.questions:
- why macrocycles with fixed bond distances contain three redundant torsional angles and two redundant bond angles ?

- What is the information regarding the rejection rate for samples where the ring torsion fingerprint deviation exceeds 0.01 before and after optimization using Equation 3?

- What's the rationale for not directly modeling non-rotatable side-chain groups like phenyl rings and instead generating them using RDKit?

- Why was the training set restricted to 30 conformers per molecule with the lowest energy as opposed to a threshold based on the lowest energy?

- I'm seeking clarification on why, in section 4.4, you claim that the additional xTB optimization demonstrates the efficacy of the diffusion scheme in achieving diverse sampling. Could you elaborate on this point?
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.summary:
The authors present their method to generate ensembles of macrocycle conformer rings. Specifically, their model takes a 2D structure for a macrocycle peptide and generates 3D coordinates in the form of bond angle and torsional distributions. 

They test their model with and without side chains in both conditional and unconditional generation. Their method uses diffusion to generate its values and ultimately serves a purpose similar to alphafold; in that it predicts spatial characteristics of the structure from the composition of bonds and atoms in the base structure. 

In figure 2, the authors demonstrate that their method can estimate characteristics measured from test samples. In table 1, they show that their method is better able to estimate these values compared to existing methods.
reviews.strengths: The authors present a novel use of diffusion to generate macrocycle peptides. They show that their method can outperform existing methods by a significant margin and is able to produce estimates quite similar to test samples.
reviews.weaknesses:
The paper doesn’t focus on its implementation details as well as it could. Most of the necessary details are there, but it also isn’t clear that their method could be definitively replicated from the details given. A system diagram or some other flowchart outlining their method could help elevate the paper. 

The paper initially gave me the impression that the full structure was being generated until this was cleared up by figure 1. 

While this is not a reason to reject the paper, I believe the paper could flow better if it was immediately clear exactly what are the inputs and outputs to their method. Additionally, they should, either in the abstract or in the beginning of the methods section, state in plain language what challenges their method overcomes that previous methods were insufficient to achieve. The authors do state what their method is generating, but the language could be improved to make their motivations clearer.
reviews.questions: The paper left me with no outstanding questions beyond certain small details which are not strictly necessary for understanding their method.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.text:
Expanded Review:

Summary:  
This paper introduces RINGER, a diffusion-based transformer model for generating three-dimensional conformational ensembles of macrocyclic peptides from their two-dimensional representations. RINGER represents each peptide in redundant internal coordinates—bond angles (Θ) and torsions (T)—and applies a discrete-time wrapped normal diffusion process to sample these highly coupled internal coordinates. A cyclic relative positional encoding in the transformer encoder enforces the ring’s rotational invariance, and a final constrained optimization (via SLSQP) ensures ring closure in the Cartesian reconstruction (Equation 3). The authors evaluate both an unconditional backbone-only version (Figure 2) and a fully sequence-conditioned version incorporating side-chain features, stereochemistry, and N-methylation (Table 1, Figure 3). Benchmarking on the CREMP dataset (35,198 training molecules, 1,000 test macrocycles with up to 877,898 conformers) demonstrates that RINGER achieves the best F1 scores for recall and precision across all-atom RMSD, ring-only RMSD (rRMSD), and ring torsion fingerprint deviation (rTFD), while requiring only a fraction of the computation time compared to metadynamics (CREST).

Strengths:  
1. Novel Model Architecture and Inductive Bias: RINGER blends redundant internal coordinate representations with a self-attention-based diffusion model specifically adapted to cyclic systems. The cyclic relative positional encoding (Equations 1–2) elegantly enforces invariance under cyclic shifts, a physical symmetry of homodetic macrocycles.  
2. High-Quality, Diverse Samples: Across both unconditional backbone sampling (Figure 2) and fully conditional generation (Table 1), RINGER matches the gold-standard ensembles generated via expensive metadynamics (CREST) in quality (low RMSD/rRMSD) and diversity (high coverage). In particular, the Ramachandran plots in Figure 3 show that RINGER recapitulates both dominant and minor modes of the ϕ,ψ distribution, whereas baseline methods produce unrealistic or overly narrow distributions.  
3. Comprehensive Benchmarks: The authors compare RINGER against a wide variety of baselines, including distance-geometry methods (RDKit ETKDGv3, OMEGA), Cartesian equivariant diffusion (GeoDiff-Macro), torsion-based diffusion (TorDiff-Macro), and a 1-nearest-neighbor retrieval baseline. They report recall, precision, and F1 for multiple metrics (RMSD, rRMSD, rTFD) as well as post hoc GFN2-xTB reoptimization results (Appendix O), clearly illustrating RINGER’s advantages in both raw sampling fidelity and after energy refinement.  
4. Practical Impact for Macrocycle Design: By reducing conformer-ensemble generation from hours (∼14 h per hexapeptide with CREST) to minutes on a GPU, RINGER makes large-scale virtual screening and inverse design of macrocyclic peptides more tractable. This speedup could accelerate optimization of key drug properties such as binding affinity, permeability, and oral bioavailability.

Weaknesses:  
1. Insufficient Implementation Details and Reproducibility: Although the paper outlines the major components of RINGER—the diffusion process, transformer encoder, cyclic positional encoding, and ring-closure optimization—many implementation specifics are relegated to appendices or described only at a high level. Critical hyperparameters such as the exact noise schedule βt, learning rate schedule beyond “10 warmup epochs,” and batch sizes are not stated in the main text. Moreover, the pseudo-algorithm for inference (sampling xT, iterative denoising, SLSQP post-processing, NeRF for side chains, RDKit for hydrogens) is not given. A clear system diagram or flowchart (beyond the brief depiction in Figure 1) would greatly aid reproducibility—particularly for researchers in cheminformatics who may not be familiar with diffusion models on internal coordinates.  
2. Clarity in Method Flow and Problem Statement: The paper asserts early on that RINGER “generates three-dimensional conformational ensembles of macrocyclic peptides from their 2D representations,” but until Figure 1B it remains unclear whether the model predicts Cartesian coordinates directly or works in internal coordinate space. A concise statement in the abstract or first paragraph of Section 3 that spells out “Input: a macrocycle molecular graph G with atom and bond features; Output: a set of angle and torsion values (Θ, T) sampled from p(Θ, T | G), later converted to 3D coordinates” would help orient the reader. Similarly, spelling out in plain language which specific challenges of macrocycle conformer generation are addressed—high torsional coupling, cyclic invariances, limited experimental structures—would reinforce the paper’s motivation.  
3. Limited Discussion of Generalization Beyond 4–6mers: The CREMP dataset focuses on homodetic 4-, 5-, and 6-mer macrocycles with canonical side chains. It remains unclear how RINGER would scale to larger rings (7 mers and above), noncanonical residues, or more complex topologies (e.g., heterodetic linkages, disulfide staples). The brief discussion in Section 5 touches on this, but concrete performance expectations or planned extensions are not detailed. A forward-looking paragraph in the main text about how the cyclic positional encoding and SLSQP closure could adapt to variable ring sizes or heterocycles would strengthen the paper’s vision for broader applicability.  
4. Flow and Readability Could Be Improved: In some places, the flow is interrupted by densely packed equations (e.g., Equations 1–2 on cyclic self-attention) or by deferring key architectural descriptions to the appendices. Reordering the material so that Section 3.1 (Problem Definition) and Section 3.2 (Coordinate Representation) succinctly state the input/output and high-level solution before diving into the detailed diffusion model (Section 3.3) would improve readability. An additional high-level summary box or bulleted list of the inference pipeline—sampling angles, SLSQP closure, NeRF reconstruction, RDKit hydrogen placement—would also help readers grasp the overall procedure and dependencies.

Questions:  
I have no major unanswered questions beyond the implementation and generalization issues noted above. A schematic pseudocode or algorithm box would reduce any remaining uncertainty about data flow and hyperparameter choices. It would also be helpful to know how sensitive RINGER’s performance is to the number of sampling steps (T) and to failure rates in the SLSQP ring-closure step (the paper mentions rejecting samples with rTFD > 0.01 but does not quantify rejection rates).

Rating:  
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold  

Despite its strong empirical results and novel architecture, the paper would benefit from additional clarity around the inputs, outputs, and implementation pipeline to ensure reproducibility and broader adoption. With the suggested improvements—particularly a clear flowchart or algorithmic description and expanded discussion of generalization—the work would be well-positioned to advance macrocyclic peptide design.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces RINGER, a novel solution for generating conformations of macrocycle peptides. RINGER is a diffusion-based model with a Transformer as its core architecture. To maintain SE(3)-invariance, RINGER operates on torsion and bond angles, and the ultimate coordinates are generated through a post hoc optimization process. RINGER is capable of performing both backbone (unconditional) generation and macrocycle (conditional) generation, and extensive experiments validate its effectiveness.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper delves into a relatively underexplored research area-conformation generation for macrocycle peptides. The proposed method, RINGER, has demonstrated commendable results in terms of both quality and efficiency, achieving satisfying outcomes in a mere 20 steps.
2. The paper conducted a wide array of experiments, providing robust evidence to substantiate the effectiveness of RINGER.
3. This paper takes into account the cyclic symmetry inherent to macrocycles and devises a novel relative positional encoding method that effectively incorporates this unique property.
reviews.weaknesses:
1. I do not think novelty is enough. RINGER shares similarities with FoldingDiff [1]. The differentiating factor lies in RINGER's introduction of a unique relative positional encoding, specifically designed to account for cyclic symmetry. It's important to note that there appears to be a lack of an ablation study on this proposed positional encoding, which could provide valuable insights.
2. Additionally, from a machine learning perspective, one may question the inherent challenges of conformation generation for macrocyclic peptides. It might be worthwhile to explore whether adapting methods from other molecule types is a feasible approach, as machine learning methods may not be strongly influenced by molecular variations.
3. In the context of unconditional generation tasks, a comparison with other existing methods would be highly valuable in order to assess RINGER's performance and capabilities more comprehensively. Additionally, in the comparison of conditional generation, where rRMSD and rTFD metrics are employed, it appears that there is a absence of a method focused on backbone generation.

[1] Wu K E, Yang K K, Berg R, et al. Protein structure generation via folding diffusion. arXiv preprint arXiv:2209.15611, 2022.
reviews.questions:
1. Why the baseline excludes Torsional Diffusion [2]? I can understand that ‘Methods such as torsional diffusion only alter freely rotatable bonds and cannot sample macrocycle backbones by design.’, but I think Torsional Diffusion can be compared ‘in the context of all-atom geometries (RMSD)’ in section 4.3 if I do not misunderstand.
2. Why GeoDiff-Macro performs so poor? Can you provide experiment details of GeoDiff-Macro?

[2] Jing B, Corso G, Chang J, et al. Torsional diffusion for molecular conformer generation. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 2022, 35: 24240-24253.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
Summary:
This paper introduces RINGER, a diffusion-based Transformer model tailored for generating conformational ensembles of macrocyclic peptides. By operating in redundant internal coordinates (bond angles and torsions) rather than Cartesian space, RINGER maintains key SE(3)-invariances and capitalizes on a novel cyclic relative positional encoding to respect macrocycle symmetry. A post hoc SLSQP-based optimization ensures ring-closure constraints are met when converting back to Cartesian coordinates. The authors evaluate RINGER on the CREMP dataset—36K homodetic macrocycles with up to 30 CREST-generated conformers each—and demonstrate both unconditional backbone sampling (Figure 2) and sequence-conditioned, all-atom ensemble generation (Table 1, Figure 3). RINGER achieves strong recall, precision, and F1 across RMSD, rRMSD, and ring torsion fingerprint deviation (rTFD) metrics while requiring only 20 sampling steps, a substantial reduction from the 5,000 steps reported for competing diffusion approaches like GeoDiff.

Strengths:
1. Important and Underexplored Topic: Conformation generation for macrocyclic peptides remains a challenging niche due to ring closure constraints and scarce experimental structures. By focusing on a modality that occupies unique space between small molecules and proteins, the paper addresses a clear gap in computational peptide design. The authors convincingly articulate in the Introduction (pp.1–2) how macrocycles are critical for binding shallow protein interfaces yet lack fast, scalable sampling tools.
2. Strong Empirical Validation: The experimental section (Section 4) spans unconditional backbone modeling (Figure 2) and conditional sequence-conditioned generation (Table 1). RINGER accurately reproduces bond-angle and dihedral distributions with low KL divergences (e.g., KL ≈0.0175 for χ1 torsions) and recovers diverse conformers on par with gold-standard CREST ensembles while running orders of magnitude faster (20 vs. thousands of diffusion steps). The post-optimization ablated in Appendix F further confirms that ring closure can be handled with minimal geometric distortion.
3. Cyclic Relative Positional Encoding: Recognizing that macrocycles lack a canonical start residue, the authors propose a bidirectional relative positional encoding (Equation 2) that captures forward and backward graph distances along the ring. This innovation effectively encodes cyclic symmetry in the Transformer’s self-attention and is critical to achieving the high sample quality shown in Figure 3, where RINGER reproduces both tight single-mode and multimodal Ramachandran distributions.
4. Practical Impact: By generating macrocycle ensembles in seconds rather than hours, RINGER has the potential to accelerate lead optimization in drug discovery. The authors highlight in Section 4.4 that RINGER’s samples require smaller quantum-chemical adjustments when reoptimized with GFN2-xTB, suggesting that the method learns chemically meaningful conformational landscapes directly from data.

Weaknesses:
1. Limited Novelty Beyond FoldingDiff: While RINGER adapts diffusion and transformer techniques from recent protein models—particularly FoldingDiff [1]—the core innovation lies in the cyclic positional encoding and redundant internal‐coordinate parameterization. The manuscript would benefit from a more detailed ablation study on this encoding, as its direct impact on performance (e.g., rTFD improvements) remains unquantified in the current ablations (Appendix N.4 focuses on positional encoding variants for open chains, not rings).
2. Questionable Generality of ML for Macrocycles: From a machine learning standpoint, it is unclear whether the highly specialized adaptations required for macrocycles (cyclic encodings, post hoc SLSQP) imply that models trained on small molecules or linear proteins are fundamentally ill-suited here. The authors might discuss if such heavy domain tailoring is avoidable—could an off-the-shelf diffusion model, suitably fine-tuned, achieve comparable results? This speaks to the broader question of how much domain-specific engineering is needed as opposed to generic generative frameworks.
3. Baseline Comparisons in Unconditional Generation: In Section 4.2 the authors present compelling Ramachandran plots for RINGER’s backbone samples versus held-out data, but they do not compare against any existing backbone‐only generative methods. Including, for example, the unconditional FoldingDiff backbone model or GeoDiff trained without side chains would contextualize RINGER’s performance gains in unconditional settings.
4. Missing Backbone-Only Baseline in Conditional Metrics: For the conditional generation metrics (rRMSD, rTFD), the evaluation in Table 1 omits a method dedicated purely to backbone sampling. While the 1-NN baseline addresses 2D-to-backbone mapping indirectly, a direct backbone generator (e.g., a torsion‐diffusion model trained on ring atoms only) could serve as a stronger point of comparison and clarify the contribution of side-chain conditioning.

Questions:
1. Torsional Diffusion as Baseline: The authors state that “methods such as Torsional Diffusion only alter freely rotatable bonds and cannot sample macrocycle backbones by design” (p.4). However, since Section 4.3 reports all-atom RMSD comparisons, could TorDiff-Macro still be included in that context? If so, please clarify why it was excluded from the RMSD-based comparison or confirm that TorDiff-Macro was indeed benchmarked for all-atom RMSD but yielded degenerate backbones, justifying its omission.
2. GeoDiff-Macro Performance Details: Table 1 shows that GeoDiff-Macro attains only 25.6% recall at 0.75 Å RMSD, despite being a strong small-molecule conformer generator. Could the authors provide details on its training regimen on CREMP (learning rate, number of diffusion steps, architectural changes) and comment on why GeoDiff’s SE(3)-equivariant Cartesian approach struggles to capture ring torsion distributions (despite its 5,000-step sampling)?

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold
The approach is solid and empirically impressive, but the novelty over related diffusion‐based methods is incremental and key contributions—such as the cyclic positional encoding—require deeper ablation. Additional backbone-only baselines would strengthen the claims. As such, I recommend marginal rejection.

Questions:
1. Why the baseline excludes Torsional Diffusion [2]? I can understand that ‘Methods such as torsional diffusion only alter freely rotatable bonds and cannot sample macrocycle backbones by design.’, but I think Torsional Diffusion can be compared ‘in the context of all-atom geometries (RMSD)’ in section 4.3 if I do not misunderstand.
2. Why GeoDiff-Macro performs so poor? Can you provide experiment details of GeoDiff-Macro?

[2] Jing B, Corso G, Chang J, et al. Torsional diffusion for molecular conformer generation. Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems, 2022, 35: 24240-24253.

Rating:
5: marginally below the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper studies the conformer generation problem in molecular machine learning. Specifically, conformer generation for ring systems is challenging for previous approaches. This paper proposes a diffusion model over internal coordinates to generate macrocycle peptide conformers. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approaches over previous methods.
reviews.strengths:
1. This paper does capture an important problem --- sampling the conformational ensembles for structures with diverse ring systems and previous effort in this direction is relatively limited.
2. This paper proposes to use a diffusion model over internal coordinates (angles and dihedrals) is technically sound and efficient to reduce the degree of freedom (e.g. distances).
3. The empirical performance of the proposed method is excellent compared to the baseline methods.
reviews.weaknesses:
The technical contribution of this paper is limited (to the machine learning community), the way to build a diffusion model over angle and torsion space has been widely studied in the related literature. IMHO, the most interesting part of the paper is about how to capture ring system conformational changes with angles and dihedrals, however, it is discussed only very briefly in Sec 3.3. How do you determine the 3 torsional angles and 2 bond angles for a macrocycle (how many atoms are in the cycle? How about a two-ring system?) The post-processing optimization step seems an effective and efficient solution to reconstruct the cartesian coordinates for the macrocycles, but how do you assemble them back into the structure (assuming you are only optimizing for the rings)?

Overall, I think this is an interesting application paper to establish diffusion models to sample conformational changes for molecular structures, especially ring systems. Given it's an application paper, I would expect more discussions from the problem formulation side and why it should be designed in this way with more case studies to demonstrate, e.g. it could handle multiple ring systems. The critical part missing is how to extract the angles and dihedrals from the ring.
